column6: Lithium Ion Battery (model: W-1,3.7v,1800mAh)
column22: I have owned a Sprint/Sierra wireless hotspot since October 2010. Yesterday I noticed that the battery door on the unit had bulged and popped off on 1 side . After closer inspection,I realized that the Lithium Ion Battery (model: W-1,3.7v,1800mAh) was extremely hot and bulging on both sides.After my discovery I immediately called Sprint and was told I could take it to Leesburg,VA store for replacement.I asked if this is a normal occurrence,and was told NO .On Sunday 1/29 I called Sprint store to be told that they don't carry  batteries.....Called Sprint again, giving my ticket # and was told that I would have to pay 35.00 for a battery,which I politely refused,as I do not feel that consumer should be responsible for this recurring problem!As I told the Sprint Rep,this appears to be a possible danger as battery could explode! She spoke with her supervisor and called me back again telling me I could call Sierra Wireless at 877-231-1144.(closed Sunday)      This is really passing the buck!Upon further research of this issue,I went to the SPRINT.COM website to discover from Sprint forums that this has been an issue since 2010.see below link:                   http://community.sprint.com/baw/message/388897    Could these batteries explode or pose a fire hazard ? Is this what it will take for Sprint to recall and replace these. I am aware that [REDACTED] had this issue as I was one of the recalls.Should these battery incidents have not been kept quiet by your company?As of Jan 30, I have spoken with a Sierra Wireless representative who is sending a battery at no charge. I am sure that if you had made Sierra wireless aware of this,  the problem may have been solved.
column38: Sierra wireless has agreed to replace battery at no charge to me as of 1/30/12 phone conversation

column6: Lorex baby monitor, Model # WL4320, 4.3 digital wireless LCD color monitor - malfunctioning rechargeable battery pack
column22: LOREX WL4320: SERIOUS BATTERY FLAW: purchased this baby monitor just under a year ago.  After a few days of use, the battery would not keep a charge (NOT safe to have a baby monitor shutting off shortly after being removed from the AC cable).  Got a replacement battery from Lorex.  A few months later the 2nd battery wouldn't hold a charge and expanded breaking out the battery tray cover (? fire risk).  Got a 3rd battery from Lorex.  Over the past week the 3rd battery is expanding again, and won't hold a charge.  CLEARLY this product should be recalled entirely as a safety risk.   Please CPSC: even if the device does not catch fire, it is a risk to infants if the monitor shuts off very prematurely when on battery power-- you have an unmonitored infant.  As a board certified sleep disorders specialist, let me also add how extremely dangerous this is, to have a completely unmonitored infant, when parents falsely believe their monitor is working.  NOTE: after contacting Lorex today to find out about getting a 4th battery, and finding out they have a technical bulletin on this, isn't that enough for the CSPC to force a mandatory recall and refund program for parents owning this flawed device?
column38: As written, multiple replacement prior lithium batteries have failed.

column39: Upon examination, investigation, and further consideration of the general issue reported related to the battery pack splitting open in a portable DVD player, it has been determined that the product and alleged issue poses no substantial risk of injury to the consumer or property damage, and thus Sony does not believe that any substantial product hazard exists.  Investigation and further examination has determined that the battery and its cells are incapable of reaching a temperature that can cause injury or property damage beyond the battery pack or DVD product itself. column9: Combination Fire/Smoke Alarm and Carbon Monoxide Detector (714)
column22: Smoke detectors with 10 year Lithium batteries intermittent false alarms due Lithium battery Voltage instability. This happened with a few Kiddie Combo Smoke/CO detectors as well as a number of [REDACTED] Sound units. The problem is with the constant falsing many people remove and do not replace the detectors and/or ignore the alarms which can and will lead to unsafe situations and someone getting hurt or worse. This does not appear to be a model or manufacturer specific issue, I firmly belive it is due to the nature of Lithium cells that are unstable and have Voltage flucuations. Even when the detectors are AC powered witht he 10 year Lithium back up battery, I beleive the battery is causing many of the detectors to false on a random basis. This is a BIG issue and need to be investigate. Just search online and you will see thouands of complaints, but many may not realize the problem is likely due to the Lithium batteries.
column39: UTC Fire & Security : At Kidde we take residential fire safety very seriously, which is why all of our products comply with the rigorous standards set by Underwriters Laboratories for performance and reliability.Nuisance alarms are a rare but possible occurrence, and have numerous potential causes that do not relate in any way to a defect in the product. We note that alarms must be cleaned on a regular basis to keep them free of dust and debris, in accordance with the instructions in the user manual and as provided on the Kidde website. They must also be placed away from vents and fans, as instructed by the user manual. Improper maintenance or placement are quite often the cause of such false alarms.  In any event, we kindly ask that the consumer contact our customer service department so that we may work with them to ensure that they are satisfied with our products, including replacing any alarms that are not meeting expectations free of charge, perhaps with a different model that may be a better fit for the customer's use. 1 (800) 880-6788 or https:///https://www.kidde.com/home-safety/en/us/support/. We also dispute the consumer's claim that the lithium battery in the unit is the cause of false alarms, as we have no evidence to support said consumer opinion. Our products must pass rigorous testing with Underwriters Laboratories for safety and reliability before they enter the market.

column6: Batteries - lithium
column22: This is a report on a [REDACTED] 2L. Wednesday night, January 23, 2013 I took it with me on a walk and it was not working right. The next morning (yesterday) I changed the 2 lithium batteries because they tested below 2 volts. I had 3 fresh Tenergy batteries & tested them before inserting them, 1 was under 3v, the other 2 were good & I put the good ones in the light. This is a 3 stage light – switch on once to get bright setting, click again & get that bulb + 3 LEDs or again & LEDs only. The first 2 stages are what quit working; the LEDs worked. When I tested the batteries I thought the LEDs might have required less power thus they kept working. But as the bright bulb didn’t light when I replaced the batteries I figured that the bright bulb could have burned out – or that the switch was bad. I brought the light w/new batteries to the office, put it on my desk. Late afternoon I checked it again, LEDs would only come on very faintly (indicating to me that something had drained the batteries since I installed them). I went to [REDACTED]’s website. Saw that this light is discontinued, but I sent an email about having it tested/repaired/replaced. About 30-45 minutes later I was on the phone at my desk when it went bam and shot a 12 flame out the switch hole and kept burning like a flame thrower. Though the flame shrunk within seconds several papers on my desk were then on fire. I grabbed the light by the strap, thank goodness for the strap because the light was extremely hot - and took it out the front door where it continued to flame for a couple of minutes. I have probably 6-10 lights with these types of batteries. I’ve gone through probably 50 batteries of this brand over maybe 6-8 years. I don’t mix batteries as they warn. I have had no problem with any other light. I’m suspicious about the switch or circuits in this one – which of course, are now cooked! This is the only light I’ve had until now that had a 3 stage switch, the rest were on/off or brief burst. I have contacted [REDACTED], they have requested the flashlight be sent in for forensic testing. They say it is a battery problem, that they are aware of these problems, but the problems are always battery, not flashlight related.

column39: NETGEAR, Inc.: NETGEAR takes product safety seriously and instructs users not to use a battery pack if its case appears damaged, swollen, or compromised. Lithium-polymer batteries do swell a small amount during normal use. Usually, a swollen battery does not present a safety risk , as the swelling is primarily from harmless carbon dioxide (CO2) gas generation. In rare instances, excessive swelling may lead to hazardous conditions. Therefore, we recommend proper disposal of swollen batteries. Users who notice swollen batteries should contact their cellular service Center for a replacement, which will be subject to the terms of the battery warranty.

column6: Google Pixel Slate Chromebook is a tablet-style device with an optional keyboard and stylus feature to mimic a laptop. This particular model is the Intel Core M3 (C1A)  version with 8GB of RAM.
column22: Google Pixel Slate Chromebook screen started to separate and expose internal components. Reached out to Google Support immediately to determine remedy. Google stated that lithium battery was swelling (bulging) and that the device was out of warranty (1 year limited) and could NOT be repaired, as none of the recommended 3rd party tech sites could source the part to replace the defective and potentially dangerous battery. Google advised to STOP using the device immediately and no longer charge it, as a swelling lithium battery poses both a fire and explosion risk, should it rupture and/or overheat. No injuries, but clearly an unsafe product, as this was not a warranty type issue (cracked screen, faulty buttons, etc.) but one where the consumer is no longer able to use the item due to bad battery design, resulting in a risk of fire/explosion if the user continues to operate the electronic device.
column39: Google: Thank you for the opportunity to review and consider this report submitted on www.SaferProducts.gov. Google is committed to designing high quality products that are both safe and effective.  Google’s products conform to all globally applicable safety standards.  When customers have an issue with one of our products, we ask that they contact Google directly to provide us with information and the opportunity to retrieve and examine the product.  This gives us information that helps us continually monitor the efficacy of our products and identify opportunities for quality improvement or required corrective action. We are in contact with the customer to understand the details of this incident and we will continue our customer outreach so we can investigate this issue further.

column6: Aeiusny Generator Model UPS-500ADThe user manual describes the product as Aeiusny Uniterrupted Power Supply.  The Amazon description provided earlier in this form included the claim that it was a solar generator.  I was using it as a standby UPS for just a modem and router.
column22: I purchased a Aeiusny 288 wh 500 W Emergency power station and UPS powered with lithium ion batteries from Amazon in August of 2019.  Aeiusny is a Japanese company with an office in Massachusetts.  I used the device in Pennsylvania.  It caught fire in my house on the evening of November 9, 2020.  I was in on a different floor when the Aieusny UPS/Generator caught on fire so I did not see it initiate.  I was fortunate to have just replaced my smoke detectors in my house so they were quick to sound off when the fire ignited and I was fortunate that I had a dry chemical ABC fire extinguisher in the room where I was using the device as a UPS with a light load (modem and router).  I was able to temporarily douse the flames on the burning generator with the dry chemical extinguisher and due to ignorance of the reaction that causes lithium batteries to burn, I unplugged the modem and router from the generator and the generator from the AC source and grabbed it by the handle with my bare hand and proceeded to run it out of my house.  It flared up again as I approached my patio door and scorched part of the door frame, the glass and burnt some holes in the screen door but I kept running a few more feet and dropped it on my concrete patio and fortunately suffered no burns but I did breath in burning lithium battery and plastic case fumes during this process.  I read later that my initial belief that I had permanently doused the lithium battery fire with an ABC extinguisher was wrong and running with it out of my house bare-handed was dangerous since it is almost impossible to stop a lithium battery fire once it begins.   Yesterday, as I was verifying that my Amazon review of the generator where I reported the event was posted, I noted that another customer reported a bigger fire event in August of 2020 where an entire room in the customer's house was burned up and the fire department was called.  So, this Japanese company had notice that their generators were unsafe yet I have not seen a recall for them.  I am sure that I and this other Amazon reviewer are not the only fires caused by their generator.  I am sure they are or at least were using the old unsafe lithium battery technology in their generators that they are selling in the US.   Amazon did remove the link to the generator in my account that I purchased in Aug 2019 so there is a possibility that Aieusny switched out the type of lithium batteries they were using to build the generator and assigned a new part number to it but they kept quiet about the danger of the prior version of the generator that they sold in the US because I have not received a recall notice from anyone about the Aieusny generator that I purchased.  I have not contacted a doctor yet about possible damage to my lungs from breathing in burnt lithium battery fumes but I am at this point in time planning on it.  I can't definitively state that I have any lung damage from my exposure to the lithium battery smoke.  In addition to the photos that I uploaded to this complaint I also have photos of the damage the fire caused to my furniture, drywall and patio door as well as a PDF of the Amazon receipt for this product.  Since they are more personal in nature I did not upload them in accordance with the directions for this electronic form.

column6: Two pack of First Alert, Smoke and CO detectors, model number PC1210V
column22: I purchased a two pack of First Alert, Smoke and CO detectors, model number PC1210V on January 6, 2020 from Costco.  About 2 weeks ago one started beeping during the day, I took it down and reset it and it appeared to work.  This morning at 1:45 AM it went off and sounded an audible alert, thank G-d there was no issue.  I removed it and attempted to disable the device.  Following instructions on device back I was unsuccessful at disabling the device (as noted by items 1 and 2 in picture below).  I broke off portion of tab noted (the bottom one).  That was unsuccessful at silencing the alarm.  I then attempted to take apart the device, it was very difficult.  I used two 3/8” long slotted screwdrivers and a hammer to break apart the device.  Fortunately, I did not hit the lithium battery and start a fire.Believe these devices should be recalled.•	I am concerned others may experience similar issues with this smoke/CO detector and be unsuccessful in turning it off.  Then they may attempt to silence it by tearing it apart or by smashing it with a hammer/mallet.  This could cause a Lithium fire.  •	Per further exploration I surmised I was supposed to tear away/break away something located a few inches from the  “DEACTIVATE” instructions and arrow.  This is noted by items 3 & 4 noted on pictures.

column6: this riding mower, each of the two mower blades is connected to its own motor by a metal rod. The visible parts of the metal rods that connect the mower blades to the motors are about an inch in diameter and appear very sturdy, but as the rods enter the motor, where they are no longer visible, the diameter of the rod abruptly tapers down to about a quarter of an inch of cheap, brittle alloy (see picture). This is a major design flaw that results in the thin alloy part of the rod snapping inside the motor, and completely destroying the motor, any time there's even a slight bump on the mower blade. My husband liked this mower so much that he didn't want to return it when one of these rods broke, destroying the motor, the very first time he mowed the yard. The company claimed it was a defective part and replaced it free of charge, so he gave it another chance. Several days later when the new mower blade motor finally arrived, my husband was able to change it quickly and easily. Unfortunately, it broke again, and again. We are on the fourth blade motor now. The cost for a new one is over $400, so this is not a minor expense. We've had this mower for less than 3 months. Cub Cadet should consider finding a new source for the blade motors - they currently buy blade motors made by Mingland Nanjing Electronics, and this company is obviously selling an inferior product.

column22: On Nov 6, 2022, our home caught fire as a result of an exploding lithium ion battery in our Dyson stick vacuum. The lithium battery overheated via thermal runaway (we always kept our Dyson plugged in) catching fire to our laundry room. The smoke, heat and water did the rest of the damage to the entire house. The fire was violent and got to over 500 degrees (enough to melt the blades of a ceiling fan). The lithium battery was not a Dyson original, but a third party replacement battery, made by a company called PowerExtra for a Dyson V7, that we bought on Amazon (it was an Amazon Choice). We lost much of our personal belongings in the fire. The house had to be stripped entirely to its' studs and the roof had to come off and be rebuilt.  We are rebuilding but won't be back in the house until Summer 2024. BTW, our insurance company, Dyson and the fire inspectors have all confirmed that the replacement battery caused the fire. PowerExtra was very unethical in their outward communications with me (post dating letters, etc). You can view a story that [REDACTED] did on our situation here:[REDACTED]

column22: While investigating a carbon monoxide alarm incident in a residence, I confirmed the presence of high levels of carbon monoxide in the home (garage) using a [REDACTED] gas monitor. When searching for a source for carbon monoxide in the garage, the only possible source appeared to be an 80V Greenworks Pro lithium ion battery that had been left on its charger for several months. There was no notable damage to the battery or charger and there did not appear to be any other problems other than the presence of carbon monoxide in the area. I returned several hours later and found no measurable CO in the home or garage, which points to the battery on the charger being the source of the carbon monoxide.

column6: It is an HP Pavilion Notebook. Product No. X3T20UA#ABA. The ruptured battery was removed and disposed of as hazardous waste. The ruptured battery was labeled as follows: HP BP02XL,  Number 849909-850,  MFG DATE 2016/12
column22: We recently encountered a dangerous situation with an HP Pavilion Notebook/Laptop (model 15-aw053nr) that nearly resulted in an explosion, fire, and personal injury. During normal use, the HP Notebook began to automatically charge its internal battery which had been latently degrading unbeknownst to the consumer. This resulted in a loud pop, a cracked notebook case and a ruptured lithium ion cell. The consumer had just seconds to unplug the notebook and place it on a non-flammable surface; otherwise a burn, fire, or explosion likely would have resulted. Since 2016, internal lithium-ion batteries made for Hewlett Packard devices have been affected by several inherent design and manufacturing issues that substantially affect the safety of consumers. The issues have prompted ongoing and repeated expansions of current battery recalls for HP notebooks. We regularly check the device's notification center and also run the complimentary HP Battery Recall Utility software on a regular basis. The previous industry design standards made it possible for consumers to easily view and proactively remove batteries if showing visible signs of degradation. CPSC needs to investigate the surrounding issues including; (1) The average consumer no longer has access to inspect the device battery,  (2) The notebook continues to automatically charge a degrading internal lithium-ion cell, potentially creating a life threatening situation, (3) the device fails to notify the consumer when the battery is failing, and (4) The notebook fails to detect and automatically cut power to a dangerous battery. We feel as consumers that the agency needs to critically review and update safety and design practices. This needs to ensure the prevention of a devestating catastophe related to these internal lithium-ion batteries, especially considering that these products are designed to be placed on or near the consumer's body. As an individual with disabilities, the consumer received help with this report.
column38: The product was not damaged or modified before the incident. Could not locate a contact method to the manufacturer for safety complaints

column6: The product was a rechargeable battery pack and it's charger...
column22: Our Fire Dept responded to a report of a fire in a lunchroom of a local bank.  On arrival, we found a small object burning on top of a microwave oven in a break room.  We removed the object and upon further examination of it, it was determined to be a rechargeable Li-On battery pack and it's charger.  The unit was left charging by the maintenance staff from use the previous night.  This was explained later to be a routine process for the last few years, since the COVID pandemic began.  There were no injuries and despite being told to leave the battery outside for the night and for additional follow-up the next day, the battery and it's charger were discarded by the maintenance staff.  Fortunately, a few photos were taken of the affected unit prior to our leaving the scene.

column1: Lithium Batteries in Electric Bikes Recalled by EV Global Motors Co.
column3: Lithium Batteries in Electric Bikes Recalled by EV Global Motors Co. NEWS from CPSC U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission Office of Information and Public Affairs Washington, DC 20207 FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE Originally Issued September 17, 2002, Last revised April 25, 2008 Release # 02-251 EV Global Recall Hotline: (800) 871-4545 CPSC Consumer Hotline: (800) 638-2772 CPSC Media Contact: Kim Dulic, (301) 504-7058 Note: Firm is out of business, and a remedy is no longer available. CPSC and EV Global Motors Co. Announce Recall of Lithium Batteries in Electric Bikes WASHINGTON, D.C. - In cooperation with the U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC), EV Global Motors Co., of North Hills, Calif., voluntarily recalled about 2,000 lithium batteries in Mini E-Bike electric bicycles. The lithium ion batteries in these bicycles can overheat and pose a fire hazard. EV Global Motors has received five reports of the batteries overheating, three of which caught fire, though no injuries have been reported. The recalled lithium ion batteries are used to power EV Global's folding Mini E-Bike. The battery, which is located in the battery compartment just in front of the seat, is supplied with most models of the Mini E-Bike. The words mini e-bike are printed on the side of the bicycle near the steering column. These bicycles are manufactured in Taiwan and the battery packs are assembled in the U.S. Bicycle, automobile and Internet retailers nationwide sold the electric bikes from February 2001 through July 2002 for between $1,400 and $1,700. Consumers should stop using these bicycles immediately. The firm is no longer in business and a remedy is no longer available.

column6: Ryobi 40 volt lithium powered chain saw
column22: Item of complaint is a Ryobi 40 volt lithium powered chain saw, ID # EU14200D070028 (approximately 2+ years old) AND  battery ID# EU16205N950422 (3 months old) began smelling of what I associate with an electrical fire/overheat.  The tool was not in use and within an enclosed garage.  When I felt of the exposed bottom of the battery (still within the tool) I found it to be hot but no evidence of flame or melting.I attempted to remove the battery for safety and the battery would not eject normally,I then attempted to pry the battery out also without success.  I then removed all of the securing screws and pried open the case whereupon I saw that the positive and negative battery terminals had welded themselves to the connecting harness.  The welded connections were then broken and the battery removed.  Testing of the battery voltage at this point indicated a residual charge of 31 volts still remaining.This episode has cost me a $200 tool and a new $100 battery, which is not significant considering the potential fire hazard that may have existed.  I would respectfully request a determination if this is an ongoing problem with this product or that my case is unusual as I have replaced both items today with a new identical product.

column39: It is Kidde’s mission to help protect people and property from fire and its related hazards. We are committed to engineering and manufacturing quality products, which is why all of Kidde’s products meet or exceed the strict third-party standards set by Underwriter’s Laboratories (UL), an independent, not-for-profit testing agency. Kidde would note that so-called nuisance or false alarms do not necessarily indicate any malfunction or failure of the unit.  In some instances, what appears to be a nuisance alarm to a consumer may be the result of an environmental condition (such as dust and debris in the unit) that results in the alarm, because it is technologically indistinguishable from an actual fire.  Accordingly, in the rare occurrence of an incident such as the one reported, we request that the customer supply us with the smoke alarm(s) identified in their report and information regarding the incident. We will then conduct a thorough examination of the alarm(s) in order to identify any potential issue with the alarm(s) and required corrective actions, if any. Our customer service manager will be in contact with the consumer so we can begin this evaluation process. We apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and sincerely thank you for your cooperation. Your assistance will help us to continue developing high quality safety products.

column22: On Monday, March 11, 2024 my son came to me and said his headlight did not work on his bike. He also indicated there was a chemical smell coming from the light. column39: Sony Electronics: With the initial information provided in this report, Sony is unable to determine yet if the product in question creates an unreasonable risk of injury or contains a defect which could pose a substantial product hazard.  However, Sony takes all such reports seriously and will investigate.  A Sony representative will be contacting the customer shortly for support Sony Electronics: Upon examination, investigation, and further consideration of the issue reported related to the battery pack splitting open in a portable DVD player, it has been determined that the product and alleged issue poses no substantial risk of injury to the consumer or property damage, and thus Sony does not believe that any substantial product hazard exists.                                                                                                                                                                                                                               The battery cells in the battery pack can sometimes swell when there is an imbalance in the charge of the cells, which can sometimes lead the battery pack to split slightly open.  However, upon investigation and further examination it has been determined that the battery and its cells are incapable of reaching a temperature that can cause injury or property damage beyond the battery pack or DVD product itself.  This is so even when an imbalance in the charge of cells occurs.   As a result, Sony does not believe that there is a safety concern or any substantial risk of injury here.  This has been reviewed and confirmed by SEL’s Director of Product Safety.

column6: 10 kilowatt home lithium ion energy storage battery
column22: Six years ago I contracted with a local solar panel installer for a rooftop solar system.  I also had them add to that system a 10 kilowatt lithium battery to provide household power backup in the event of a power grid failure.  The battery was from LG Energy Solution and came with a 10 year warranty(Model RESU 10H).     Three years later LG advised that my battery was part of a lot that was subject to a recall it was coordinating with the CPSC, due to a risk of the battery overheating and causing fire and harmful smoke.  It promised a replacement within a few months, but it actually took two years.  I see that LG was involved with CPSC three separate times for this recall, in 2020, 2021, and 2022.     About a year later - Thursday, August 1, 2024 - I was summoned to the basement by a smoke alarm, to discover the replacement battery overheating and smoking.  Luckily I was able to contact the solar installer by phone and he guided me through shutting down the system, which cooled down the battery.  He came the next day to remove the battery, and one of the same ones he had installed as part of a solar system for my son.     I am furious that a major company like LG would subject me or anyone else to this kind of risk, long after it became aware of the problem and said it had been fixed.  It is apparent that despite the documented risk of fire with this model, LG is issuing replacements of it instead of its newer models.  These older models constitute a clear and present danger of fire, without warning, to any household in which they are installed.  I would like LG to be forced to recall all of these batteries for anyone affected, and also refund the $7000 purchase price for me and my son.  Neither of us desire to have another backup battery from LG at this point.
column38: After the fire the solar installer removed it and is storing it for me.

column6: This was the product description as of 2019-2020 on amazon.com GETIHU Portable Charger, Ultra Slim 10000 mAh Power Bank, 4.8A High-Speed 2 USB Ports Battery Pack with LED Flashlight, Compatible with [REDACTED]
column22: On Saturday, November 14th, 2020, our household had a GETIHU lithium ion battery explode in our kitchen. The battery made a pop noise and then there was a small flame. At the point where my husband tried to carry the battery out of the house (through a sliding glass door) with a spatula, it then fell on our hardwood floor and began spinning and spewing out ash and sparks. My husband was finally able to contain it by putting it in a bowl full of water. The damaged battery and two similar batteries now reside in a metal container on our driveway (concrete) out of fear of further combustion.I want to report this because when I made the manufacturer aware of this through their customer service email, they have repeatedly tried to get me to send the damaged battery back to them in China via USPS (illegal). After my concern about illegally sending this damaged battery, they then suggested that I send it to a hub in California via UPS (I know that UPS allows for shipment of damaged lithium ion batteries under certain circumstances). However, they do not have the proper permits for ground shipping via UPS and they have not provided me with a safe container for transport. Moreover, they would be putting *my* name as the return address, making me liable for any damage that could happen en route.GETIHU has pulled their listing of this product from amazon. com. I purchased one battery on August 18. 2018 and two similar batteries in June of 2019 (June 5 and June 22). The listing read as: GETIHU Portable Charger, Ultra Slim 10000 mAh Power Bank, 4.8A High-Speed 2 USB Ports Battery Pack with LED Flashlight, Compatible with [REDACTED] After the battery exploded, I posted a note of warning to purchasers on Saturday, November14/ Sunday November 15 to the only listing for that battery that was still up on amazon. The listing has since been pulled from amazon. The manufacturer now sells another battery in its place, but I believe has not placed any warning to consumers of the batteries I purchased.My concern is that there were many batteries of the kind I purchased that were sold in 2018-2019. I do not feel comfortable knowing that this incident happened to me and could happen to others in the future.NOTE: The battery that combusted was unplugged/not-charging at the time and had been unplugged for over 10 hours. Previously it had been plugged into an outlet to charge overnight (necessary to get full charge) using a standard generic charging cable. In fact, two of these batteries had been charging in the same outlet with similar cables and were subsequently removed from charging after showing full charge. Only one of the batteries combusted.

column39: Sony has reviewed this report. Sony engineers have previously identified this service issue and determined that in rare cases a swelling of the internal battery cell pouch can occur which can result in the two halves of the battery pack’s plastic enclosure to separate, without a compromise to the cell pack other than the swelling. The blower was placed out of service and the battery removed.  The unit was inspected by a supervisor and the battery that was in use was found to be working properly and function normally in other battery power tools.  A charged battery was reinstalled in the leaf blower in question and with electrical isolation gloves and isolation from ground, the leaf blower was tested.  The leaf blower no longer worked and did not appear to be providing any electrcial energy through the plastic handle.  The leaf blower remains out of service at the below address.  The employee continues under a doctors care and six days later has limited use of the right hand.

column22: We use the Wav Hello Sound Bub portable bluetooth speaker and soother (Belle the Bunny) for our daughter during naps and nighttime sleep. The instructions say that for continuous sound, leave the machine plugged in, so that's what we did. However, after this incident, I read the warning, which states that it contains a lithium battery which can overheat if plugged in for too long. This information should be clearly stated in the instructions, as it is contradicting. I took pictures after that I can provide if you need them. Thank you.

column22: What happened that made you want to report?  Dell XPS 15 9570 laptop touchpad popping up physically, did not function correctly. [REDACTED][REDACTED][REDACTED]    I disassembled the laptop and removed the battery.  It is swollen, photo attached to this report.  Once battery was removed, touchpad returned to correct physical position and was functioning correctly.  Photos attached to this report.   Laptop operating on AC power pak only.   No battery.    What type of product(s) were being used?  Dell Laptop XPS 15 9570.  Purchased brand new online from Dell in Dec 2018.  Who was using the product(s)?  I was using the laptop and experienced issues with the touchpad.  It was not responding correctly to touch.  Also it was popping out of its physical space, rising above keyboard.   How were the product(s) being used?  Using laptop to run applications needed for work.  Also traveling for business carrying laptop onboard airplanes and various modes of transportation. column22: 3 weeks ago tomorrow, August 24th, at about 7:20 am EDT there was a strong smell of burning electrical wires  in my house. I quickly walked around the house and then realizes outside the door from my Florida room to my pool I could see dark smoke. I ran to that door and as I was going out,  there was a loud explosion to my right where the covered screen porch is. M17 month old EGO blower was on the wood floor in the middle there, next to my spin bike and stack of new patio chair cushions, with flames were shooting from the top of it. The fire was already spreading to cushions. I pushed the blower away from the cushions with a tall pool filter that was nearby. Then I grabbed the blower by the long end and threw it in the pool. All this happened in less than 1/2 of a terrifying minute. After about 1/2 an hour I fished the blower out of the pool with a 2x4 and set it on the concrete poolside. The battery started to blink red !! So using the 2x4, I got the blower out to the middle of my crushed stone parking area, afraid the battery might explode again. 2 days later I wrapped the blower in a silver tarp and took it back to Lowes. they were sorry for my experience, and gave me a gift card fur the full amount of the blower. I walk on the beach every other morning at 7 am, and think it's very lucky that I was home when this happened. I live alone since losing my husband. and because of this EGO battery, I could have lost my home and my 3 beloved cats. I have told all my family members to be a lot more cautious where and how their lithium tool batteries are stored. I have always been mindful not to over charge batteries, or leave them on the chargers; but I NEVER thought a newish battery that had not had a lot of use , just sitting there attached to a tool could cause such mayhem

column39: Microsoft Corporation: Microsoft takes this and all reports regarding its products seriously.According to our records, this device was first activated on February 29, 2016 and we have no records for repairs or exchanges. The customer then purchased this device off eBay sometime in January 2019 without a warranty. After submitting his report to the CPSC, the customer did go into a Microsoft Store and received a replacement device. Regarding the safety concern expressed by the customer, some Surface customers have reported expanded batteries in their devices. Like all tablets and laptops, Surface devices are designed to be portable. Most expanded battery concerns reported to Microsoft involve Surface devices with fully charged batteries that are plugged into AC power or docked for prolonged periods of time (e.g., 24/7/365), with batteries that are rarely discharged. Charging for prolonged periods of time and rarely operating a device on battery power can cause the batteries to expand.Batteries that have expanded as described are not an inherent safety concern. In such cases the expansion is caused by the formation of non-flammable carbon dioxide (CO2) gas. However, Microsoft nonetheless encourages users to exercise care to avoid puncturing an expanded battery while handling a device. Like alkaline and other batteries, lithium ion batteries contain caustic materials that can cause injury if the materials come into contact with skin, eyes, or lungs.For customers who have experienced an expanded device battery, we recommend that they:•	Stop using the device and disconnect it from the charger;•	Not open the device or attempt to force the screen back into place;•	Not compress, puncture, or tear the battery pouch; •	Avoid touching any exposed portions of the battery; and•	Dispose of their device in accordance with local e-waste requirements.We trust this information will address the customer’s concerns. Please feel free to contact us if we can provide additional information. Microsoft Corporation: Microsoft takes this and all reports regarding its products seriously.Microsoft received the customer’s Surface Book at our product safety lab; it was examined by a product safety engineer on August 9, 2019. The engineer determined, consistent with our earlier response, that the device exhibited an expanded battery which could be observed near the upper half of the display screen. The information provided in response to this report remains the same. As noted in our earlier response, the expanded battery concerns that have been reported to Microsoft involve devices with fully charged batteries that are plugged into AC power or docked for prolonged periods of time (e.g., 24/7/365), with batteries that are rarely discharged. column6: Google / Nest Hello (wired) video doorbell.Model Nest Hello (wired)UPC 813917020944
column22: I purchased a Nest Hello video doorbell approximately 3 years ago.  This summer, it started rebooting when the button was pressed and not ringing the doorbell.After searching on-line, I found out there is a lithium ion battery in it that is not documented.  It's sealed in to the product with heavy tape, and not able to be inspected.Google, Nest is one of their product lines, will not repair a Nest doorbell when it is out of warranty.  Since the product was not repairable by Google, I ordered a new battery from a reputable supplier, and opened the doorbell.   The battery in it was not only defective, but was swollen, like the ones about to catch fire.   I saved the battery in a safe place (bucket of sand, outside.)  Not having a way to inspect a potentially hazardous battery either physically, or via software and not notifying customers of this dangerous situation is the core of my complaint.In the event of a fire, the doorbell is physically attached to the outside of a house, with low voltage wires penetrating the wall to the inside of the building.  This could  lead to an extremely hazardous situation for the customer since smoke detectors would not detect the initial onset of fire.Customers might also not be able to address this situation because of: 1: Not being aware there is a dangerous battery in the product. 2: Not being able to see the problem since the battery is sealed inside the product. 3: Not being notified via software that there is a problem with the battery. 4: Google will not repair the Nest Hello doorbell when it is out of warranty.  This refusal to repair it extends to replacing the battery.I avoided a fire by being proactive and fixing a product that the manufacturer will not support after warranty.  Others might not be so lucky.
column39: Nest Labs, Inc.: Thank you for the opportunity to review and consider this report submitted on www.SaferProducts.gov. Nest is committed to designing high quality and effective products that help people stay safe in their homes. When customers have an issue with one of our products, we ask that they contact Nest to provide us with information and the opportunity to retrieve and examine the product. This provides us information that helps us continually monitor the efficacy of our products and identify opportunities for quality improvement or required corrective action.  The customer seems to have disassembled their doorbell in order to replace a small battery sealed into the weather-tight enclosure.  Such product disassembly is not recommended as the disassembly process could damage the battery or other internal components. The customer discovered that the small battery appeared swollen and was concerned that it posed a hazard.  However, the device is designed to remain safe even as the battery ages, as it seems was the case here.  We have contacted the customer to resolve this issue and to ask that the device be sent to us for laboratory analysis. Thank you again for your feedback, and we apologize for any inconvenience you have experienced.

column22: On 3-2-20, our insured [REDACTED] plugged his HP Pavillion Laptop Charger into a receptacle prior to going to bed. As he descended down the stairwell, he observed what appeared to be smoke coming from the den area which is the room they'd just left for bed and where the laptop was located.  As he got to the bottom of the steps, the smoke detector started going off.  When he entered the den area, he noticed a strong electrical type smell/odor and fire coming from the bottom bookcase where the laptop was left charging. He reported the fire to the [REDACTED] Fire Dept. and they came out to ensure everything was extinguished.  A report was prepared.  He contacted his Home Owner's Insurance Carrier, [REDACTED] to report a claim.  After the First Notice of Loss was prepared and it was assigned to an Adjuster, the Adjuster contacted Sr. Special Investigator [REDACTED] who works that County.  Origin and Cause (O&C) Inspections were conducted on March 5th and the 10th.  We determined the Cause of the Fire to be a sudden explosion of the lithium batteries.  The battery was a replacement purchased on [REDACTED] and not an HP battery.  The battery was purchased on November 28, 2018, Brand: Bull-Tech K104 replacement.  It appears Bull-Tech is located in Henan, China and sells products via 3rd party platforms [REDACTED] and [REDACTED]. column22: My daughter was riding a Mototec 48V electric dirtbike and received a severe laceration on her leg. She was sitting stationary on the bike and then applied the throttle. The bike lurched forward and caused her to fall to the left. Her speed was very low, maybe 2~3 mph. The fall itself caused a small sharp bracket on the outside of the kickstand to puncture her leg. When she rotated her body to get up from the fall, the bracket that had punctured her leg then continued to slice open her leg. Her leg was severely lacerated through all layers of skin, about 5 inches in length. After examining the bike after the incident, the bracket is in a location that will contact the leg during a fall, with the weight of the bike on that bracket. The bracket is also stamped steel and is very sharp on the edge. This is a safety defect. I noticed on Mototec's website that the bracket has been relocated to the inner side of the kickstand on new models shown on their website. CPSC need to investigate Mototec to consider recalling these products and preventing other injuries.

column6: SimpliSafe Entry Sensory - this contains  CR-2032 Lithium 3V batteryFCC ID: U9K-ES3001 IC:20992-ES3
column22: My home uses a SimpliSafe home security system. The intention of this is improved safety. This evening, one of their entry sensors became displaced from a window in my toddler's bedroom, just by me gently knocking into it while pulling down the window's shade. The sensor came apart (though the pieces did not break, the device's components just separated), and within it was a lithium ion button battery which came out and fell to the floor with the sensor and separated from it easily. My husband, who installed these devices, could not initially locate the button battery and asked for my help in finding it on the floor of my child's bedroom given the dangers we know it can cause if ingested. We located the battery, and given how easily it became dislodged from the sensor, I promptly removed them from the room. I have contacted the Simplisafe company to give them feedback on this. This product is not in line with the CPSC laws and requirements for button battery containing devices. Luckily no harm came to anyone but if the battery was ingested by a child, damage could have been serious and deadly. I am a pediatrician myself (in addition to being a mother), and know the severity of button battery damage and risks of ingestion. Recently, Reese's Law was put into place by the CPSC to avoid such injuries.
column39: SimpliSafe, Inc.: The safety of our customers is a top priority for SimpliSafe. We appreciate customers providing this type of feedback because we are committed to providing safe and high-quality products. SimpliSafe is in compliance with Reese’s Law, though the unit involved in this report was installed well before the law took effect. Nevertheless, included with every entry sensor is a back cover/mounting plate with pre-drilled holes and compatible screws for permanent mounting of the sensor. Any customer wishing to permanently install their entry sensor is invited to contact SimpliSafe at 1-800-297-1605 to request the mounting screws. Customers may also contact us with any questions they may have about their products.

column6: SASA Tech Flashing LED Wireless Headphones
column9: Stereo or Hi-fi Components or Accessories (546)
column22: Wireless headset sold in children's section of Walmart is using defective lithium battery packs that are likely to explode or light on fire while on a child's head or while charging. The SASA Tech WM-422 headphones (SASA USA 525 7th Ave, New York, NY 10018) stopped charging properly within days of purchase (the charging status light on the headphones stopped lighting up while charging, and the amount of run time kept getting shorter and shorter, until reduced to mere minutes). Something was obviously wrong with this headset, so I checked Walmart's website for reviews to see if this was a common issue. Almost immediately, I noticed one review with photos which showed they had the battery pack in their headphones expanding so badly that it popped the headphone casing/earpiece apart, and it was quite obvious that their battery was imminently threatening to rupture. So, worried our headphones were suffering similarly, I carefully pulled the earpiece off of our headphones and discovered the same thing... what appears to be a very puffy, swelled up foil pack, that is clearly an inferior/defective lithium battery... it is plain to see that it has expanded/puffed up and is getting ready to pop/explode/ignite/etc. I'm no expert, but I did not notice any apparent physical or electronic protective elements to keep the battery safe (to prevent improper charging or punctures or help contain a rupture) like one sees with quality lithium batteries or well designed devices which contain them. This headset was only manufactured last year; it seems pretty wild for this to be happening already not only in a set of headphones, but ones with light-up LED exterior lighting, sized for children's/adolescent's heads, and sold in the children's section of the largest retailer in the USA. A product clearly intended for children, of Chinese origin, using seriously dangerous battery packs with flagrant disregard for said children's safety... simply awful. The company's website appears to be a shell devoid of any real substance,  merely some pictures/vapid marketing gibberish and a form at the bottom to submit to purportedly contact them. The product's instruction manual list no other contact information except the website and address. These things are dangerous and need recalled, just wild.

column22: On Sat, March 7, 2015 at approx. 2:30pm., I discovered  my garage fully engulfed in white smoke,I immediately called 911 and  Montgomery County MD Fire Dept.(MCFD) responded. Upon arrival, the cause of the smoke was determined to have been  a Ryobi 40 volt lithium battery charger located on top of a refrigerator in the garage.  It should be noted the charger was NOT plugged  in and  battery had last been utilized  in Oct. 2014 prior to being cradled in the charger for the winter.MCFD determined the charger overheating had caused  the resulting small fire/smoke to burn  theplastic (charger/ battery)  and  paint (top of refrigerator). MCFD utilized  a heat seeking device to locate the  source of the fire/smoke after first ruling out the refrigerator plug as the cause. The charger/battery were destroyed and refrigerator damaged. Additionally, smoke damage wasevident  behind  the  refrigerator, which leads directly into the family room  of the residence. MCFD also  indicated  that  the  timing of my opening  the garage  doors after smelling  the smoke in the family room  limited the fire/smoke damage from spreading to other locations inside the  residence. I purchased the Ryobi charger & battery  in Sept. 2014 from The Home Depot as part of a Ryobi Expand It grass trimmer/ tree pruning kit.On  March 7,  at approx. 3:30pm., I  contacted Ryobi Customer Service and  the CPSC to report  the incident. I was told by Ryobi that the incident was being forwarded to their Safety Division and that a Safety Officer would contact me within 48 hours. On Mon., March 9 the CPSC contacted me; however, I have not been contacted by Ryobi as of this writing (March 10 & 11) to no avail. Ryobi customer service will not allow the consumer to speak with the Ryobi Safety Division. The weather conditions were dry, approx. 40 degrees, with snow on the ground.

column22: I had a Surface Book that, through normal use, developed screen yellowing due to excess heat. Turns out the heat was coming from the li-ion battery, which eventually heated and swelled up to the point that it a) melted the glue that kept the screen inside the bezel and b) pushed the screen out of the bezel on that side, bulging it out and leaving a considerable gap.The battery had been swelling for a very long time before it got to this point, making it impossible to close the laptop flush anymore.A swollen and overheating li-ion battery is a huge risk that may have led to an explosion, and it likely had been leaking hazardous gasses!Microsoft was no help at all when I reported the issue, and offered nothing but a discount on another same-model  Surface Book, with no guarantee that it wouldn't be bringing the same hazard into my home again.

column39: We at Lenovo are concerned about any report of a safety issue involving one of our products.Upon learning of alleged incident, Lenovo's product safety and customer care teams reached out to the Submitter of the Report in order to (1) confirm that a Lenovo product was involved; (2) verify the facts relating to the alleged incident; and (3) obtain the hardware so that we might perform diagnostic testing in an attempt to ascertain what, if anything, occurred and if additional measures may be required.Lenovo was able to make contact with the Submitter of the Report, and requested that the battery be sent to Lenovo at our cost for analysis.  To date, the battery has not yet been received.Since the system that is alleged to have been involved in the incident reported was not purchased directly from Lenovo or a Lenovo authorized reseller, we are unable to confirm at this time that it was, in fact, a Lenovo product or that Lenovo authorized parts were involved in any failure.  From the information provided in the Report, however, it appears that the system is approaching ten (10) years old.  We have no record of service based on the part number provided, can cannot confirm whether the subject battery was an original part (which , given the age of the system is unlikely) or a replacement part authorized and approved for use in the subject system by Lenovo.  Based on the information provided, it appears that neither the battery nor the system identified in the Report have been the subject of any Lenovo recall, and are unaware of any history of defect or other internal issue with systems or batteries of that vintage.   Without the subject hardware we are unable to verify the facts in the Report or understand what failure, if any, or other factors may have contributed to the conditions the Submitter reported.  We will continue our attempts to obtain the subject hardware from the Submitter of the Report so that we can perform testing and any resulting analysis of the alleged incident to determine if additional measures may be required.

column6: The smoke alarm is manufactured by Kidde, model P3010H, a photoelectric smoke alarm with a 10-year lithium battery. According to the label, it was manufactured July 5, 2014. I purchased it from a large retailer and installed it in March, 2016.
column22: The smoke alarm is manufactured by Kidde, model P3010H, a photoelectric smoke alarm with a 10-year lithium battery. According to the label, it was manufactured July 5, 2014. I purchased it from a large retailer and installed it in March, 2016. Not quite one year after activation, the alarm sounded in the middle of the night. The hush button did not function as advertised. The detector was removed to another room and stopped sounding.Today, it sounded again in the middle of the night (of course!), the hush button had no effect,  and was removed to another room, where it stopped. Two hours later, while on a table in another room, it sounded again. No response from the hush button. In each instance, none of the other five alarms in my home sounded (one is an identical model). I had a 35-year career in the fire service, and now six more years as a licensed and certified expert witness in fire investigation, and I'm pretty confidant that there were no products of combustion in the home during any of these alarm activations.A quick check on the internet found numerous instances of consumers complaining of the same issues with this model alarm. No information about recalls was found.Here is my concern: consumers dealing with this same issue might choose to deactivate or discard their smoke alarms as a false alarm is unsettling and annoying. If they do this, they will no longer have the protection of smoke detection in their homes. Over my fire service career I took part in investigating more than 60 fatalities from fire, and it is well known that the single most important thing that any of us can do to prevent a fire death is to have a working smoke alarm in our homes.Additionally, if there is an issue with false alarms, I have no confidence that the detector will actually detect smoke. I have not yet tested this unit, but will if you choose not to.I request that the CPSC investigate and evaluate this model smoke alarm to determine if there is any wider problem than my own detector failing. My faulty detector is still operational and available for evaluation and testing if necessary. I have, however, removed it from my home and will replace it before the end of the day.Thank you for your consideration, and thank you for the work that you do to keep us all safer.[REDACTED]
column38: I submitted an online request to determine my rights under the manufacturer warranty.
column39: It is Kidde’s mission to help protect people and property from fire and its related hazards. We are committed to engineering and manufacturing quality products, which is why all of Kidde’s products meet or exceed the strict third-party standards set by Underwriter’s Laboratories (UL), an independent, not-for-profit testing agency. Based upon the description provided, and without the actual alarm at issue, we cannot determine what may have occurred.  In the rare occurrence of an incident such as the one reported, we request that the customer supply us with the alarm identified in their report and information regarding the incident. We will then conduct a thorough examination of the alarm in order to identify any potential issue with the alarm and required corrective actions, if any. Our customer service manager will be contacting the reporting customer so we can begin this return and evaluation process. We apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and sincerely thank you for your cooperation. Your assistance will help us to continue developing high quality safety products.

column22: This morning I woke up to chirping of my 4-5 year old google nest protect carbon monoxide and smoke detector (manufactured June 2016). I then got a notification that the batteries needed to be replaced. The batteries are the original Energizer lithium ion ones that came with the unit. I got out the ladder and pulled the protect from the ceiling. I could immediately detect the smell of burning plastic / electronics and felt the back of the unit was hot. When I pulled out the batteries, one row was cool and the other was very very hot - I thought I might burn myself and dropped the batteries they were so hot. Using a old shirt, I moved the batteries onto a shelf, but I'm worried the might catch fire (thinking about all the lithium ion battery problems from a few years ago). Very scary.I contacted the manufacturer and they sent my case to their safety department. They promised to get back to me.This seems to be a recurring problem (the word hot appears as the 3rd [REDACTED] search suggestion when searching for nest protect batteries). There are many forum reports of the problem in googles own online forum as well as other forums. Phone support said this was rare, but that clearly isn't the case from the simple [REDACTED] search. All the other threads are handled privately so it is difficult to know what we should do to protect ourselves from the nest protects. I'm very worried that one of these fire detection devices is going to cause a fire!Thanks, [REDACTED]

column6: Device name: FrostySerial number: 010578771057Surface model: Surface Book i7Version Information	Embedded controller: 90.2226.256.0	SAM: 90.1837.256.0	UEFI: 92.3192.768.0	Touch driver: 107.3.94.3	Wi-Fi driver: 15.68.17018.116
column22: My Microsoft Surface Book had the lower left corner separate. Nothing had happened to cause this issue. No damage or other problems. After researching what was happening I came to find that it is a known problem that Microsoft has with this product. I contacted Microsoft's customer service to be told that it would cost me $600+ and that the unit being sent would be refurbished and may or may not have the same issue. I was also informed that, This issue may be caused by an expanding battery on you surface, there is no inherent risk or safety concern associated with these expanding batteries. With that said, like alkaline and other batteries, the lithium-ion batteries used in surface devices contain material that can injure skin, eyes, and lungs only if punctured. We recommend to our customers not continuing use of a device with an expanding battery, except where the surface operates on only battery power and for the purposes of data transfer. Surface devices with expanded batteries should not be connected to AC power as it could possibly lead to rupture. And I find myself questioning the statement, there is no inherent risk or safety concern associated with these expanding batteries.The representative stated that they would work within the Microsoft structure with a consultation about waiving the fee. The next day I received an email that stated, I hate to be the bearer of sad news, but after all my efforts yesterday and today, I was not able to get another option for you with the consultation we had on the chat, about removing the fee of the replacement. The only option that we have available for you right now is the one I mentioned in the chat. Not only do I feel like Microsoft knowingly has sidestepped the concerns and known issue for years. It seems like they are wanting to make money off of the very issue they caused. For a company of that size and scope to be okay with products being flawed and not informing the consumers is reprehensible and irresponsible.

column22: While using a Brother mobile duplex scanner model DSmobile 920DW / DS-920DW for short, I went to move the scanner sitting on my desk by grabbing hold of the right side when I was surprised to discover that it was extremely hot and instantly let go.  I unplugged the USB cord from my laptops charging / connection port and allowed over an hour for this electronic device to safely cool down, showing no sign on the outside of being damaged.  I opened the battery compartment on that same side to discover that the Brother rechargeable Battery (Li-Ion) 3.7V 1700mAh  6.29Wh Model LD0665001 (still being sold) was expanded inside the battery slot that took some effort to remove.  Sitting in a storage bag for this mobile scanner were two additional Brother batteries, one which was already expanded.  I reported this to Brother CS/Technical Support and was assigned incident [REDACTED].  Brother claimed battery is no longer being sold by them, their own website says differently.  I sent a reply before knowing this that so far has not been replied to.

column22: For this item: RC Plane 3 Channel Brushless Foam Glider Plane, 40 kmh Remote Control Plane - Fly Vertically, Inverted & Tumble, Large RC Airplane Toy for Adults Kids Beginners Boys Gifts (Amazon Link: https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D8KF13YJ?th=1)This toy almost caught on fire on my first attempt to use it!! I have been an avid RC user for years. Various kinds, planes, helicopters and cars. Most RCs share the same few battery connectors, which all are impossible to plug in backwards. I have used lithium batteries in this hobby for atleast 10 years, and I have never had any issue with the batteries, never had one catch fire.This on the other hand, caught fire within a few minutes of me opening the box! You can see the pictures I posted. The battery connector is very flimsy and you can EASILY plug it in backwards. This happened to me and it started smoking heavily and melting the foam and the battery connector. The battery quickly got very hot. Luckily I was still holding the battery and I imminently realized what was happening and unplugged it before the battery itself exploded. This tells me two things, the battery connector is faulty and there is no polarity protection on the plane itself, which would have prevented this entirely. Polarity protection is industry standard. Houses can be burned down and people die from this simple oversight. I would not get this for any child, let alone a 14 year old that the box says it is for.

column22: Laptop Battery exploded and started a house fire. Report below is available: EXECUTIVE SUMMARYFire Investigator [REDACTED] delivered the evidence for the above loss [REDACTED]Berkeley office on September 6, 2017. The following items, identified per chain ofpossession, were collected from the residence at the above loss location:DP-#1) HP laptop CPU and chargerDP-#1a) Laptop batteryDP-#1b) Charger and debris from fireDP-#1c) Sprinkler head[REDACTED] of [REDACTED] inspected the evidence on September 13, 2017. Theevidence was non-destructively examined visually and through radiographic imaging. Scenephotographs were also reviewed. Based on the non-destructive examination and the reviewof scene photographs, the most likely ignition source for this fire loss is the energy releasedfrom the HP laptop battery pack. There are several possible causes of failure of thesebatteries including: manufacturing defect, overheating, overcharging (of specific cells), ormechanical damage to the battery cells.This brief does not intend to fully outline my investigation. At your direction, a moredetailed report will be provided. Select photographs and radiographic images are embeddedin the body of the report. Additional photographs and radiographic images will remain in myfile and will be available upon request.[Redacted] Page Claim 2 # [REDACTED]LABORATORY SUMMARYDP-#1) HP Laptop CPU and Charger:Photo 1: Photo depicting the incident laptop and charger remains from DP-#1.This evidence item consisted of an HP Pavilion g6 laptop and HP laptop charger. Thecharger exhibited directional and external damage. The wall side cord was severed just afterexiting the A/C to D/C converter box. This severed wire had beads on th e stranded copperconductors consistent with an electrical arc suggesting that the charger was plugged into anelectrical outlet. It is unknown at this time if the other end of the charger was connected tothe laptop. A radiographic image of the chargerPhoto 2 (left) and 3 (right): Photos depicting the charger remains from DP-#1.Photo 3 shows the beaded electrical conductors on the severed wall side cord of the charger.[Redacted] Page Claim 3 # [REDACTED]The laptop appears to have been closed during the fire. Most of the damage is concentratedat the battery compartment at the bottom rear of the laptop and to the left side of the screen.Additionally, there is a section on the bottom of the laptop, remote from the batterycompartment, which appears to have peeled open from heat. There are pieces of copper foilstuck to the bottom of the laptop which appear to have been ejected from a lithium -ionbattery. These battery ejections could have caused the peeled open section on the bottom ofthe laptop. A radiogrthe laptop. The laptop batteries were not included in this evidence item.Photo 4: Photo depicting the bottom of the incident laptop from DP-#1.DP-#1a) Laptop Battery:Photo 5: Photo depicting the lithium-ion battery array from evidence item DP-#1a.This evidence item contains six 18650 sized lithium-ion batteries connected in series whichwere in turn connected to a small printed circuit board. The size of this ba ttery array appears[Redacted] Page Claim 4 # [REDACTED]consistent with the size of the battery compartment of the laptop from DP-#1. The positiveterminal end caps from two of these batteries were missing along with a portion of theirinternal contents. A radiographic image of these batteries revealed that the density of thetwo batteries with the missing end caps was far less than the density of the remaining fourbatteries, which confirms that a portion of the internals of those two batteries were ejected.Photo 6: Photo depicting the missing end caps from two of lithium-ion battery array fromevidence item DP-#1a.Image 1: Radiographic image depicting the lithium-ion battery array from evidence item DP-#1a.Note the two batteries on the left with significantly lower density and missing end caps.[Redacted] Page Claim 5 # [REDACTED]DP-#1b) Charger and Debris from Fire:Photo 7 (left) and 8 (right): Photos depicting the debris from evidence item DP-#1b.This evidence item contained the remains of a three-conductor power cord and a various firedebris. Upon review of the scene photographs, the power cord serviced a soldering iron thatas reportedly not plugged in at the time of the fire. Melted plastic on the distal end of thecord suggests indeed that it was not plugged in at the time of the fire. Among the debris ofthis evidence item were pieces of the laptop monitor, bits of copper foil, and other piecesfrom the laptop. The pieces of copper foil are most likely part of the internal componentsejected from the lithium-ion batteries from DP-#1a.DP-#1c) Sprinkler Head:Photo 9: Photo depicting the sprinkler head from evidence item DP-#1c.[Redacted] Page Claim 6 # [REDACTED]This evidence item contained a sprinkler head. This sprinkler head appears to have activated.The sprinkler head also exhibited signs of mechanical damage most likely caused by firedepartment overhaul and/or evidence collection. This sprinkler head was rated for 160° F.CONCLUSIONBased on a non-destructive examination of the evidence collected from this loss and thescene photographs, the mostly likely cause of the fire in this case is a fault or failure of theHP lithium-ion battery pack leading to the ejection of the contents of two of the battery cellsand the combustion of the HP laptop.A search of the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) website revealed that thereare three separate recalls for HP Pavilion battery packs, two of which had been expanded intheir scope (one was expanded twice). Because of the damage to the battery pack identifyinginformation, it is unclear at this time if the battery pack was included in one of these recallsor their expansions.Recommendations:[REDACTED] recommends performing destructive testing on the evidence collected inthis case to further refine the ignition scenario hypothesis. In addition, [REDACTED]will be placing HP on notice of this loss and inviting them to have their own expert attendthe destructive testing.Our assessment of this case is based on an analysis of the physical evidence and anevaluation of all possible causes. Please do not hesitate to contact our office if you have anyquestions or if we can be of further assistance.

column6: It is an electrically powered recliner that has a lift function, where the chair tilts slightly forward and the seat lifts up to assist in exiting the chair, plus a wide range of recline positions, including one where a user's feet can be elevated higher than their head.
column12: The Zero Gravity Lift Chair by Relax the Back
column22: My disabled wife got trapped in an electric reclining chair and was unable to get out of it due to a failure of its battery backup system.The chair is electric powered lift chair/recliner and it provides for a wide range of positions, from tilting forward and lifting up to assist in physically getting out of the chair, to an extreme recline position that allows the user to elevate their feet above the level of their heads.  It only operates electrically, cannot be operated manually, and has an 9-volt battery backup that is meant to provide enough power to return the chair to a normal upright position in the event of a power interuption.  On one occasion the power to the chair was interrupted while my wife was in an extreme recline position.  To her distress, the battery backup failed to provide power to reposition the chair, and my wife, who has very limited mobility, was trapped and unable to get out of the chair.  In my opinion, this constitutes a safety hazard, particularly for the disabled, who likely are prime customers for this chair.After contacting the retailer, then having a service technician change out numerous components of the chair in an unsuccessful attempt to repair it, I contacted the chair manufacturer.  After speaking with someone in the engineering department, I was told that they were aware that the 9-volt battery backup system didn’t work, but that they would have an improved lithium battery backup system coming out on future models.  I asked about any plans to warn existing owners of the chair and retrofitting them with the newer backup system and was told that they had none.  They said they had notified retailers about the problem, but my retailer said he had never received such notice and was still selling the current line of lift chairs believing that the current battery backup system worked.Getting trapped in such a chair is frightening, uncomfortable, and potentially dangerous for anyone with limited mobility.  I think the manufacturer should notify each chair owner of the problem and supply them with the new lithium battery backup system.  Alternatively, they could recall all the chairs and issue refunds.My wife’s experience seems very similar to that described in Incident Report [REDACTED] from 11/10/2020.
column39: Golden Technologies: Golden Technologies did become aware of this on our more technologically advanced chairs.  We have since added a Lithium Battery Backup system.  For the PR130, this change happened in July of 2022.  All communication was given to our staff and retailers.

column39: This person has contacted us about this incident. We have asked this person to produce a receipt, invoice, or any other identifying evidence to show proof or purchase or photo proof that the product in fact is an authentic product that is manufactured by us. The person has not been able to produce any such evidence to show where and when he purchased the product or photo evidence to show that the product he used is an authentic product manufactured by us. We had offered to replace his product in good faith as long as he was able to provide some kind of evidence to show it is our product. The reason we asked for proof of purchase and proof of authenticity of the product, is because there are many counterfeit knock-offs of our product being sold in the marketplace. Our company in the last year has filed suit against over 20 different companies that have infringed on our patents. That is why it is absolutely crucial for this person to provide us with proof that the product was authentic and purchased from one of our authorized resellers and within warranty. Furthermore, this person had mentioned to us as well as in their submitted report to CPSC that they used the lithium jump starter product as a battery tender charger for their car battery. If in fact the product in question is manufactured by us, then that is a clear admission of misuse and abuse by this person. Our product is stated very clearly on the packaging, in the user manual, and our website and marketing materials that our products are car battery jump starter only and is not a charger. There are also very specific details of warning included on the product, in the included user manual, on the packaging, and an included warning card in the packaging that states very clearly it should be disconnected immediately once the engine has been started. This person states that they connected the product to their car battery and left it to charge it. Again if the product is in fact ours, then that is not the intended use of the product and is clear misuse and/or abuse by this person.

column1: Nikon Recalls Rechargeable Battery Packs Sold with Digital SLR Cameras Due to Burn Hazard

column1: Razor Recalls Self-Balancing Scooters/Hoverboards Due to Fire Hazard
column3: This recall involves non-UL certified Hovertrax brand self-balancing scooters, commonly referred to as hoverboards. Hovertrax hoverboards have two wheels at either end of articulated dual platforms and are powered by lithium-ion battery packs. The recalled Hovertrax brand hoverboards have serial numbers beginning with 55X120-01, 55X120-02, 55X110-01, 55X110-02 and 55X230-01. The serial number is printed on a white sticker on the bottom of the deck or on the charger.

column1: Victory Innovations Recalls Electrostatic Sprayers with Lithium-ion Battery Packs Due to Fire and Explosion Hazards
column3: This recall involves Victory Innovations and Protexus-branded cordless handheld and backpack electrostatic sprayers used to disinfect surfaces. They come with a nozzle, nozzle wrench, tank, lithium-ion battery pack and a battery pack charger. The Victory Innovations cordless sprayers have a green and white exterior. The Victory Innovations logo appears on the front or the side of the green and white sprayers and model VP-20A or VP-20B appears on the battery pack. The Protexus logo appears on the front or the side of the black and white sprayers and model PX20A or PX20B appears on the battery back. Battery packs on recalled units have visible screw heads and a case with no parting lines.
