column9: Stereo or Hi-fi Components or Accessories (546)
column22: My Husband and I purchased a waterproof bluetooth portable speaker, it arrived unmarked and in prime condition-- or so we thought. I opened the package the speaker was in and realized it was dead, so I used the charger it came with to begin charging it. Within two hours of charging the speaker exploded. There were two blasts- one from the device itself and one from the lithium ion battery within it. My husband got it unplugged before the 2nd lithium ion battery exploded. We rushed to get our children outside, then the pets outside, then we began ventilating the house. Thankfully my husband was able to use cardboard to extinguish the flames from burning too much in our house, but he is suffering for that now. My family and I are now suffering chemical fire inhalation, we have bloody mucus, eye/skin irritation, gastrointestinal symptoms, respiratory problems, central nervous system dysfunction, and I worry about the long term health effects this will have on our children-- who were in the vicinity when the device exploded. The air inside our house is still reading poor and the voc count has been the highest I have ever seen it for almost 7 days now. After calling poison control not many could help in knowing what to do when exposed to something like this, so now we are attempting to keep up with medical care while trying to figure this all out. The company has been hesitant to work with me and has off-put communications. This has been extremely difficult for my family and I; and we do not want this to happen to anyone else. We will never be the same from this.

column22: The item in question is a Kobalt 80-volt Brushless Lithium Ion 21-in Cordless Electric Lawn Mower, sold through Lowe's.  The mower was purchased on/about 16 Sept 2018 and used approximately 6h since.On 23 Sept 2018, after using for approximately 1/2 hour, the mower was laid on its side for cleaning.  A hose was used to wash off loose grass clippings from the underside of the mower.  I had not removed the battery.  Less than one minute into the cleaning, the mower started spinning at full speed.This mower is equipped with a safety lock-out button on the handle which must be depressed prior to the bail handle being pulled in order to engage the blade.  Neither the lock-out button was pushed nor the bail handle pulled at the time the mower started on its own.I include a link from YouTube of another consumer who reports a similar incident:    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HL-hOO3GoOQThis incident scared the living daylights out of me.  If any body part or animal had been in the vicinity of that blade, it would have been sliced to ribbons.It appears from my experience and that of the person who posted on YouTube that water on bottom of the mower can override the mower's safety lockouts.  This is a VERY dangerous product that has an extremely high potential of causing traumatic injury.
column38: This is a VERY dangerous product.  I plan on letting them know.

column6: Greenworks 21 inch, 60 volt lithium-ion, brushless motor, cordless mower. Model M060L00.
column8: Lawn Mowers, Leaf Blowers and Lawn Vacuums
column22: My electric mower started spontaneously and without warning. I tipped the mower on its side to clear away already wet grass under the deck and was using water from a garden hose; I had not removed the battery yet. The blade went to full speed only inches from my hands. I had to place the mower back on its wheels and pull out the battery to get it to stop. I reinserted the battery and the motor started again without having to pull back the bail lever and/or press the safety lock-out button as usually required. I repeated this several times with the same result. The red safety key next to the battery was the only other way to stop the mower motor. I have a 572 MB video of this process. The mower is relatively new; purchased in June of 2017 and used about 6 times. Each use consumed only 1 indicator bar on the battery. That is about 20 minutes of run time x 6 total use. A warning in the manual says not to expose mower to water to reduce the risk of electric shock but does not mention unexpected or spontaneous starting of the motor. It also states to remove the battery before cleaning but again does not warn about unexpected or spontaneous starting. I am certain others will do exactly as I did and will suffer permanent injuries. This is a major safety issue and should be addressed. I checked the mower today and it appears to have returned to normal. It is obvious now that the water somehow got into somewhere it should not have. I was not injured and am not asking for compensation of any sort. This was truly a Close Call for me, a couple of inches would have change my life forever. You can be sure I will pull the battery at the drop of a hat from now on.

column22: The Lithium Ion Rechargeable Battery Pack ( NP-FX110 ) for our Sony DVD player has had one of it's cells expand to the point that it has cracked the plastic case that houses the cell.  It is pressurized to the point that it seems like it will explode if anything punctures the cell's membrane.  The unit is now inoperable.It's safe to say that this is happening with MANY other units and this is not a fluke occurence but a manufacturing defect that could potentially hurt someone.  Sony has to realize that these DVD players are primarily used by children and a curious child whose DVD player stopped working could potentially puncture the pressurized cell, exposing the child to its chemicals.I'd like to know what Sony will do about this and if they are interested in keeping a long time customer.
column38: I would like Sony to contact me.
column39: Upon examination, investigation, and further consideration of the issue reported related to the battery pack splitting open in a portable DVD player, it has been determined that the product and alleged issue poses no substantial risk of injury to the consumer or property damage, and thus Sony does not believe that any substantial product hazard exists.  Investigation and further examination has determined that the battery and its cells are incapable of reaching a temperature that can cause injury or property damage beyond the battery pack or DVD product itself. As a result, Sony does not believe that there is a safety concern or any substantial risk of injury. This has been reviewed and confirmed by SEL’s Director of Product Safety.   Sony has attempted to contact the customer to evaluate the product, but has unfortunately been unable to reach the customer and has not had an opportunity to verify the claims in the report or conduct an examination.

column22: Purchased a 12 piece set of lithium ion batteries from Amazon Sept, 9, 2016.Have used them many of times in a strap on head lamp running a [REDACTED] LED light.Driving my car at 10pm saturday 6/3/2017 had removed the device from on my head and set it on the center consul of my car.   Began to hear a hissing sound I thought was emanating  from my car radio.  As I was adjusting the volume control on my radio which had no effect on the noise I was hearing, I realized the sound was something else.  At that moment a bright flash and loud bang filled the cab of my automobile.  What had exploded in my car blew shrapnel into both of my eyes making it impossible for me to keep my eyes open.  Fortunately I was on a familiar road and was able to maintain control of my vehicle and pull off the roadway.  I am a volunteer firefighter and carry a medic kit and bottled water which I immediately began flushing my eyes with.  After 20 minutes on the side of the road i regained partial vision and was able to drive my car the 2.5 miles back to my house.  My eyes hurt through the night but after about 12 hours returned to normal and the pain went away.  Upon inspecting my vehicle the next morning discovered my windshield had been shattered from the force of the explosion.   I contacted Amazon and informed them of the incident and they immediately refunded the entire purchase from last september and referred me to contact the seller and the CPSC.  I have had no response from the seller in California except to say he would let the manufacturer know.  My request for the name of the manufacturer was not complied with and all other letters sent have gone unanswered.  including my demands for repair of my car's windshield which has duct tape holding it together because I a, on Social Security and can not afford to replace a $450 windshield.

column6: Brickbrite (see www.brickbrites.com), a light up lego-like toy
column22: I wanted to warm you that my children (8 and 4) managed to break one of these Brickbrites (www.brickbrigtes.com) open (I think it was thrown and hit the floor).  It opened at the seam between the upper plastic portion and the lower plastic portion.  The unit has three small button batteries in it.  It took us about ten nervous minutes to locate the button batteries that had come out.  As you probably know, these button batteries can be extremely dangerous for smaller children.  Please see the article in Pediatrics at this link for more details about the dangers of button batteries:  http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20498173Thanks,   [REDACTED], MD-------------------Pediatrics. 2010 Jun;125(6):1168-77. Epub 2010 May 24.Emerging battery-ingestion hazard: clinical implications.[REDACTED]SourceNational Capital Poison Center, 3201 New Mexico Ave, Suite 310, Washington, DC 20016, USA. toby@poison.orgAbstractOBJECTIVES:Recent cases suggest that severe and fatal button battery ingestions are increasing and current treatment may be inadequate. The objective of this study was to identify battery ingestion outcome predictors and trends, define the urgency of intervention, and refine treatment guidelines.METHODS:Data were analyzed from 3 sources: (1) National Poison Data System (56535 cases, 1985-2009); (2) National Battery Ingestion Hotline (8648 cases, July 1990-September 2008); and (3) medical literature and National Battery Ingestion Hotline cases (13 deaths and 73 major outcomes) involving esophageal or airway button battery lodgment.RESULTS:All 3 data sets signal worsening outcomes, with a 6.7-fold increase in the percentage of button battery ingestions with major or fatal outcomes from 1985 to 2009 (National Poison Data System). Ingestions of 20- to 25-mm-diameter cells increased from 1% to 18% of ingested button batteries (1990-2008), paralleling the rise in lithium-cell ingestions (1.3% to 24%). Outcomes were significantly worse for large-diameter lithium cells (> or = 20 mm) and children who were younger than 4 years. The 20-mm lithium cell was implicated in most severe outcomes. Severe burns with sequelae occurred in just 2 to 2.5 hours. Most fatal (92%) or major outcome (56%) ingestions were not witnessed. At least 27% of major outcome and 54% of fatal cases were misdiagnosed, usually because of nonspecific presentations. Injuries extended after removal, with unanticipated and delayed esophageal perforations, tracheoesophageal fistulas, fistulization into major vessels, and massive hemorrhage.CONCLUSIONS:Revised treatment guidelines promote expedited removal from the esophagus, increase vigilance for delayed complications, and identify patients who require urgent radiographs.PMID: 20498173 [PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE] Free full textPublication Types, MeSH TermsLinkOut - more resources

column13: Ryobi Cordless Jet Fan Leaf Blower Model # P2108
column22: Dear Consumer Products Safety Commission:On July 5th I sent Ryobi Tools the following email:Tonight July 5, 2017 my wife was operating our Ryobi One+ 18-Volt Lithium-Ion Cordless Jet Fan Leaf Blower (Model # P2108, Serial # EU16291D080162) that we bought at Home Depot in October of 2016 when the orange plastic fan inside the unit disintegrated into pieces which discharged through the housing and the electric motor started smoking. My wife immediately threw it down and ran. To say the least, she was shaken up, but fortunately not seriously injured. I have been a New Jersey attorney for over 35 years and can tell you this is definitely a defective product - for the exact same reasons as the electric one you previously recalled (MODEL [REDACTED]). You are on Notice, and this defective product should be recalled immediately before anyone gets injured by flying fan pieces or the electric motor catching fire or lithium battery exploding as a result. I just called this in to your 800-860-4050 # (Refer to Customer # [REDACTED]) and your representative told me to take it to a repair center in Somerville or Bound Brook be repaired. That is not acceptable! For the same reasons as you'll see below with your other recall, I demand a replacement; or a full refund for our cost of the blower, the 2 lithium batteries, and the battery charger. I checked in April or May and this unit was NOT of the Model # or in the range of serial #s of blowers being recalled. See Recall Date: March 2, 2017, Recall Number: [REDACTED], One World Technologies Recalls Electric Blowers Due to Laceration Hazard. But I now see that they were being recalled for the same defect.Hazard: The plastic fan inside the blower can break causing the fan blades to be discharged from either end of the blower tube, posing a laceration hazard. Remedy: Replace Consumers should immediately stop using the recalled electric blowers and contact One World Technologies, Inc. for a free replacement. [REDACTED]Please confirm to me though your Safety Department whether I am to receive a replacement leaf blower or a refund; and how this leaf blower product defect is being addressed.Thank you. [REDACTED]_______________________________Because this defect has already caused personal injuries in their electric cord model of the exact same design,  and because I’m receiving no response, I have called Ryobi numerous times only to be told by their Customer Service people that my complaint is at the Products Safety Department in a stack of complaints and will be tended to on an “as received basis which should be in 10 to 14 business days”.  They refused to escalate it nor allow me to talk to anyone in their Safety Department.  This is irresponsible and outrageous.  I ask that you folks contact them and request that they recall this product before more people get injured.  Thank you.
column38: Ryobi refuses to escalate the matter in their Safety Division even though the electric cord version of the exact same leaf blower with the exact same plastic fan was recalled due to personal injuries from the same fan blade failure we experienced.

column22: There is a known and ignored safety issue with the batteries on Future Motion's OneWheel Pint X. The battery box that contains the large, dangerous lithium ion battery has sharp edges that crimp the battery wires. This can cause errors, the rider to be thrown off the board, or fires. Every Pint X that has been opened appears to have damaged wires. They are boards that show damage upon opening the box that just after arrival directly from the manufacturer. Future Motion refuses to recall, refund, or repair the root cause of the issue. They are assuring customers there is nothing wrong, but also told me in an email Our leadership is investigating the production of new battery units to ensure they are built safely and within tolerance. They are clearly aware that there is an issue, but are refusing to take action. This is all being documented at [REDACTED]

column22: I charged my brand new Philips One Blade razor using the provided cord.  When I went to take it off after it had finished charging, the light to charge it was not illuminated all of a sudden.   I took the device off and it was extremely hot to the touch and the battery has caused the plastic around the bottom to expand and push the pieces apart due to an overload of the battery or a failure of international circuitry.  The battery compartment was hissing with a low volume high pitched almost whistle like noise and emitting a slight foul odor.  This could cause burns to someone, chemical exposure to Lithium Ion battery components.  Outlets tested and are providing correct current and voltage specified by manufacturer narrowing issue down to product itself.  The device had been charging for less than one hour.

column6: RY401014US Self Propelled Lawn Mower
column8: Lawn Mowers, Leaf Blowers and Lawn Vacuums
column22: I was using my Ryobi RY401014US lawn mower this weekend and it suddenly stopped. I was mowing dry short (less than 3) grass. I opened the battery compartment to check if it the battery died and noticed flames and smoke from the vents. I quickly pulled out the red key and removed the battery to avoid any dangerous lithium battery combustion. It seems like the defect was enclosed within the unit and it was not related to the battery. I closed the battery lid and pushed the mower to safe spot and waited for the flame and smoke to stop. No immediate injuries, but did inhale a good amount of smoke trying to remove the battery and key. If the battery were to overheat or cache fire I could see this as a very dangerous life threatening concern.

column22: I own a portable Sony 9-inch DVD player (model number NP-FX110) which, to my surprise, stopped functioning suddenly. It wasn't dropped, spilled on or put in the sun. column39: Upon examination, investigation, and further consideration of the issue reported related to the battery pack splitting open in a portable DVD player, it has been determined that the product and alleged issue poses no substantial risk of injury to the consumer or property damage, and thus Sony does not believe that any substantial product hazard exists. Investigation and further examination has determined that the battery and its cells are incapable of reaching a temperature that can cause injury or property damage beyond the battery pack or DVD product itself. As a result, Sony does not believe that there is a safety concern or any substantial risk of injury. This has been reviewed and confirmed by SEL’s Director of Product Safety. A Sony representative will be contacting the customer within the next few days.

column6: PRODUCT IS A CARBON MONOXIDE DETECTOR DESIGNED TO BE PLUGGED INTO AN ELECTRICAL OUTLET WITH A RECHARGEABLE (BELIEVE, LITHIUM BATTERY) BATTERY BACKUP.
column22: A neighbor inquired about our carbon monoxide detector which had been sold to us by our heating and air-conditioning service company.  The contractor had plugged it in approximately 2 years 5 months ago into an outlet in our kitchen and the unit had not been unplugged since.  I unplugged the unit and was shocked to find the electrical outlet and faceplate were scorched and the back of the unit had scorch marks around its plug and some of the plastic had melted.  I took the unit to our local fire department and a fireman confirmed that the unit was about to burn our house down.  He was unable to get the casing off as it had melted.  I then took the unit to our air conditioning and heating contractor (a large local firm) where the own was horrified when I showed him the unit.  He said they would contact the Carrier Corporation and, of course, replace the unit without cost.  He said they had sold many of these units which have rechargeable, built-in batteries and he is very concerned.

We are committed to engineering and manufacturing quality products, which is why all of Kidde’s products meet or exceed the strict third-party standards set by Underwriter’s Laboratories (UL), an independent, not-for-profit testing agency. In the rare occurrence of an incident such as the one reported, we request that the customer supply us with the alarm identified in their report and information regarding the incident. We will then conduct a thorough examination of the alarm in order to identify any potential issue with the alarm and required corrective actions, if any. Our customer service manager will be contacting the reporting customer so we can begin this return and evaluation process. column22: I received an email from Home Depot saying a hedge trimmer we had purchased somewhere between May 2019-April 2020, Not sure of the exact date, has been recalled for a faulty trigger. I was injured back on May 27,2020, when I went to place the trimmer on the ground. Somehow it went off  when I laid it down and cut my left index finger and my left middle finger and top of my palm. It happened so fast I wasn’t sure what had happened. I thought maybe I was just careless somehow. I really didn’t think much about it until I received the recall on it yesterday. My husband drove me to ER where they stitched it up but I had to be transferred to a bigger hospital to see a hand specialist. I had to have surgery to repair my nerves and tendons in my index finger and part of my middle finger. I am still not able to flex my index finger on the end and my whole finger is numb and part of my middle finger is also numb to touch. My index finger bothers me all the time with tingling and not being able to bend it on the tip. I have not used the hedge trimmer since then.

column22: I purchased a 30,000 mAh lithium-ion portable battery bank called the Zerolemon Tough in Dec. 2018 off Amazon that is advertised as drop/shock-resistant. On 11-24-2019, after charging the power bank to 100% the device spontaneously combusted into flames in my bedroom after being dropped from a height of less than 3 ft on to a carpeted floor. The device intitially spewed electrical smoke for 10-15 seconds and then burst into flames quickly thereafter. column6: Micro-Start lithium Ion Battery Auto jump starter and multi-function electronic equipment chargeror power supply.
column22: On August 3, 2018,  at about 3:45 PM we were driving back from a hike in the Valles Caldera National Monument on High 4 near Los Alamos, N.M. when my wife heard a pop in the rear of our SUV and our vehicle immediately filled with dense white smoke. My wife yelled stop the car and everybody get out. I was driving and thought the vehicle had caught on fire. Our son, my wife and grandchildren got out of the stopped vehicle immediately. Our son raced to the back of the SUV and opened the rear door which released a cloud of white smoke and one of our dogs. The other had jumped over two rows of seats and out the front passenger door. Our son saw the smoking Micro-Start XP-10 on the floor in the back of the SUV and grabbed a strap on it and threw it from the vehicle to the ground. It was still smoking. We had pulled over on this two lane road through the forest and were fearful the Micro-Start XP-10 might start a fire. We called the fire department and asked for help. They and the police arrived to direct traffic and inspect our vehicle and the Jump-Starter. We had thrown it into our emptied cooler on ice packs and covered it with water. The firemen determined it was out and no longer a fire hazard then told us the smoke from the Lithium Ion battery was toxic. He suggested we monitor our dogs as they were close to the source longest and if they became sick we should seek care for them and ourselves. The firemen suggested we have the interior of the vehicle scrubbed out and replace the air conditioning unit air filters after we got home. We notified Antigravity Batteries by phone and left a V.M. about the episode and also sent an e-mail the night of August 3rd. They responded via e-mail August 6th and answered my phone call to the warranty desk. They e-mailed a Micro-Start Warranty Claim Form which we completed with all the requested information. We provided receipts for the expense of cleaning the SUV interior and the A/C Filter replacement. We have not shown any ill effects to the smoke nor have our dogs. The initial moving smoke filled vehicle and emergency exit from it could have resulted in more serious consequences.

column22: This incident occurred on March 3, 2024 when using the Bissell Pet Icon Model 22889. The incident was then reported to Bissell customer service on March 4, 2024. This email documentation with pictures of the vacuum was provided on March 11, 2024 at the request of Bissell customer care.When using the vacuum, the unit started to produce a burning smell and smoke was visible. The vacuumthen turned off and would not turn back. The decision was made to take the vacuum outside as there was a risk of fire. The vacuum was excessively warm/ hot the touch on the back cover. The back was removed to ensure that there was no fire or continued treat of fire. The following was found the vacuum had experienced an internal thermal event resulting in the wire and battery plug melting and fusing together. The battery pack was not disconnected from the vacuumcontrol board.

column22: On September 18th 8:06PM a fire occurred in my garage caused by a defective SafPow 42V 2A Battery Charger. The charger was connected to electric scooter and malfunctioned, resulting in a thermal runaway event that led to a fire. The incident caused significant property damage, personal injury, and financial losses.Sequence of Events:1. Fire Origin:The SafPow 42V 2A Battery Charger was actively charging the [REDACTED] electric scooter at the time of the incident.The charger overheated and caused the scooter's battery to ignite, resulting in a rapidly spreading fire.2. Spread of Fire:The fire destroyed a [REDACTED] electric scooter and caused severe damage to three additional scooters stored nearby:[REDACTED]Despite attempts to mitigate the damage, all four scooters were declared total losses.3. Personal Injuries:While attempting to move the burning [REDACTED] scooter out of the garage to protect my family, I sustained multiple injuries:Broken Nose: The burning scooter struck me in the face, resulting in a nasal fracture requiring splinting and pain management.Burns to Both Hands: First-degree burns were sustained while handling the burning scooter.Facial and Lip Injuries: A deep gash above my upper lip (now a permanent scar) and a bitten-through lower lip requiring antiseptic treatments and healing ointments.Inhalation of Toxic Fumes: Prolonged exposure to smoke and hazardous fumes emitted during the fire.Impact and Losses:Property Damage:The fire destroyed all four electric scooters, leaving no salvageable parts. The estimated replacement cost for the scooters is $8,898.Medical Costs:Out-of-pocket expenses for personal injury care include burn care supplies, wound treatment, nasal fracture care, and protective gear, totaling $165.Emotional and Physical Strain:The injuries and incident have had a lasting impact on my physical health, emotional well-being, and ability to conduct daily activities, including managing my business.Attempts to Resolve:I have made extensive efforts to contact Amazon regarding this incident. These efforts include speaking to numerous supervisors and managers, being referred to their partners such as [REDACTED], and repeatedly following up with their claims adjusters. Despite my persistence:The claims adjusters have ceased responding to my inquiries.Supervisors and managers have been unwilling to engage in direct communication or provide clarity on the resolution process.Responsibility has been shifted to the third-party seller, despite Amazon's role in facilitating the sale and the product's inclusion in a global recall due to severe fire and burn hazards.Additional Note:The SafPow 42V 2A Battery Charger involved in this incident was recalled one week after the fire, underscoring the danger posed by this defective product. The lack of accountability and resolution has exacerbated the financial and emotional toll of this incident.

column22: Reporting on Kidde ionization smoke alarm model i12010SFour of these were installed May 2017. Two have failed, the most recent occurrence 5/24/18. That's a 50% failure rate. In both cases, false reports of fire occurred despite the fact that the alarms have been well maintained by vacuuming and periodic application of canned air as per manufacturer. This is a 10 year Lithium battery backup model as well as being AC powered. In both cases the manufacturing date is 2016 Nov. 10.  The house installed in is clean, well ventilated, and the alarms are properly installed following mfg recommendations. Environmentally, Relative Humidity reported by the weather service was at 68% with a house temperature noted at 67 degrees F. I'd like to stress again, a 50% failure rate is absolutely unacceptable for a product designed to provide fire safety!

column22: On August 15, 2019 I had a lithium-ion battery pack explode while in the charger which subsequently caught my house on fire. The battery exploded into a number of individual cells which continued to explode one-by-one starting several separate fires in my garage. It took two full fire extinguishers to put out the multiple fires over the course of 10 to 15 minutes . The battery pack had just been purchased from Amazon.com and installed into the charger about 6:30 pm on 09/14/2019. The battery pack exploded at 10:30 am the next morning 09/15/2019. The battery pack was an after-market direct replacement for a [REDACTED] cordless drill and sold as such. The battery pack was manufactured in China.  I had fire damage and some property damage to my garage. If my wife and I had not been home at the time, or had the battery exploded during the middle of the night while we were sleeping the entire house would have gone up in flames. It was very fortunate that we heard the explosion and that I had two fire extinguishers ready nearby to quickly put out the multiple fires. I’m also lucky that I was not seriously injured while doing that since I only had shorts and sandals on at the time. I only sustained small burns on one toe and the top of my left foot where I came into contact with some of the hot metal or plastic while extinguishing the fires.  There were lithium-ion cells still exploding while I was trying to extinguish the flames.  I reported the incident to Amazon’s customer service department shortly after the fire that same morning and they immediately removed the product for sale from their online store and refunded my purchase, but that’s all they would do. I’ve spent almost three hours on the phone during 3 separate calls and have been given different instructions and responses each time. In the end I received an email from Amazon’s Executive Customer Relations department stating that Amazon takes no responsibility for any damages or losses caused by merchants selling through the Amazon marketplace...and that I could contact the merchant in China (through their merchant link) if I wanted to pursue the issue further with them.One more thing - there have been a number of incidents of lithium-ion battery fires aboard commercial and package carrier aircraft, I’d like to see some regulation against Amazon (and other online only retailers) to force them to take liability for selling counterfeit and poor-quality after-market products through their online store. If I had purchased an OEM battery from a brick-and-mortar store or directly from the OEM (even if the OEM product was manufactured in China) both companies would be liable for the damage and losses caused by their product. Why does Amazon get a free pass on incurring any liability for the products that they sell? That regulation needs to be changed.

column22: The screen of my Microsoft Surface Pro 4 tablet is bulging with batteries showing,  gets hot when you use it and the screen is is discolored in the lower left hand corner.I contacted Microsoft and was tolde  We have received feedback from our Microsoft Surface Extended Team stating that there are a number of possible reasons that a lithium-ion battery can swell. The most common cause is an overcharge of the battery, which causes a chemical reaction between the electrodes and the electrolyte, resulting in the release of heat and gases that expand inside the battery, causing the casing to swell or even to split open.  We recommend that customers not continue to use a device with an expanded battery, except where the Surface operates only on battery power and for the purposes of data transfer. Surface devices with expanded batteries should not be connected to AC power as it could possibly lead to rupture.  Please contact your local municipality for guidelines on how to dispose of your device responsibly as the guidelines may vary depending on where you live.  This product is still being used and sold on various sites online such as [REDACTED], [REDACTED], etc. . Apparently it could potentially cause a fire or injury if the batteries and screen rupture. Consumers were not notified of the potential problem. I registered my product with Mirosoft when I purchased it, but was NEVER notified of the problem and possible exchange. Now they tell me it it beyond the time to exchange the devise.  Had I not [REDACTED] the topic, I would not be aware of the potential hazard. There are many consumers out there with this defective and potentially dangerous product. Microsoft refused to recall or repair this problem . They tell you not to use it and get rid of it. I have about  $1,500 invested in this product and I should just throw it away? I used this product when I was hsopitalized and when I traveled. Needless to say with the the onset of Covid , my traveling ceased. We were notified of a severe storm and possible loss of electric which is the reason I decided to charge the devise. This product needs to be recalled before it blows up in some child's face or catches someones home on fire.
column38: Feedback from Micros Surface Team RE: lithium battery can swell. Most common cause is an overcharge of the battery, which causes a chemical reaction causing batteries to expand and screen to swell or split open. Advise not to use . No Fix for problem.

column6: DeWalt DCC5670B   6OV CHAINSAW DeWalt DCB606     Flexvolt 6 AH batteryDeWalt DCE100B  20V blower with a DeWalt DCB205 5ah  battery Used both chainsaw and blower but batteries on the chargers and one of them started the fire .
column22: 42 year old male in ohio was using a DeWalt Flexvolt 60v chainsaw and DeWalt 20v blower. He used  the tools for 4 hours off and on.Then took them into the garage when the blower battery died. He plugged both batteries onto their chargers. Then went inside to make dinner. Shortly after my meal He saw smoke coming from the direction of my garage.He called in the fire and fortunately his garage was fairly empty and the tools were on a metal bench. The fire was extinguished and the melted mess was disposed of by the fire department. Their was smoke damage to the garage and all his power tools were melted by a DeWalt lithium battery. He contacted DeWalt with the Original purchase reciept and they still haven't replaced his tools. DeWalt customer service repetitively transferred him after requesting information. He didn't even ask for recompense for the smoke damage only replacement of the tools lost.

column1: Pelican Power Brick Battery Charger Recalled for Fire and Burn Hazards
column3: The recalled Pelican Power Brick is an external recharging battery pack that sells as a separate accessory to be used with the Sony PSP™ (PlayStation Portable). The Pelican Power Brick consists of two lithium-ion battery cells connected via a circuit board and mounted in a small plastic housing. The battery cells are charged when plugged into an electrical outlet using the PSP™ AC adaptor. Once charged, it is used to recharge the PSP™. The model number for the battery charger, PL-6018, can be found on the back of the unit. The battery charger was sold alone and also as part of several kits containing other accessories by Electro Source LLC. Sony neither manufactured nor distributed the Pelican Power Brick. Only the Pelican Power Brick is included in this recall.

column1: Haibike Recalls Electric Bicycles Due to Fall Hazard
column3: This recall involves Haibike XDURO Urban, Race and Superrace models of electric bicycles. The recalled bicycles have an aluminum frame, hydraulic disc brakes, and a lithium battery. The recalled bicycles have a letter/number combination ('HAERA' followed by at least three numbers and characters) printed on the front of the frame. Remove the battery to locate the letter/number combination on the frame. The following models are included in the recall. column1: Brunton Outdoors Recalls Battery Packs Due to Fire Hazard
column3: This recall involves Brunton's Impel and Impel 2 rechargeable, portable battery packs that are used to charge cell phones, tablets, laptops and other devices. The Impel battery came in a rubberized shell in dark gray with orange or blue and the Impel 2 in light gray with black trim. The battery packs can be plugged into an A/C wall outlet, a 12 volt car charger or an attachable solar panel for recharging. They measure about 7.5 inches long by 7 inches wide by 1 inch thick. The lithium ion polymer battery packs have 16, and 19 volt outputs and a USB port. The Impel model also has a 12 volt output. Brunton is embossed on the top of the battery pack, along with the power button and five LED lights.

column1: Lexmark Ventures Recalls Solexio Hair Stylers Due to Electrocution or Shock Hazard
column3: This recall involves lithium-ion battery packs and replacement batteries on XS series Pulsator, Swimmer, This recall involves Solexio STYLR Hair Stylers, which can be used to straighten hair. They are black with either a pink or gray colored handle, controls and lock switches with matching color on the power cord. They are approximately 11 inches long, 1.4 inches in diameter and weigh about 14.5 ounces. Product number 78T0100 for pink models and product number 78T0300 for gray models is printed on a black and gray label inside the barrel of the stylers. Affected models have a date code between 147 and 253. The date code is the middle three numbers of the serial number, which starts with 8081. The serial number is located below the product number on the silver and black label inside the barrel of the stylers.

column22: I bought a Finchley electric recliner from Macy's in July 2019.  On Thursday, December 10, 2020 in the middle of the night the chair had a huge POP that scared the heck out of me and woke me up. There was a strange electrical burning smell.  I unplugged the chair and had my son who is a Firefighter come over to check it out.  The cord was melted and he believes the battery is what created the loud bang.  I can no longer use this chair; however, I am so grateful my house did not catch on fire!  This is SO unsafe!!!

column22: This caller is reporting her Lithium Charger manufactured by manufacturer in Bentonville, Ark and purchased from retailer in Wetumpka, AL 36092 on November 28th, 2014.Around April 15th, 2015 the caller and her husband had their charger plugged into an outlet but not charging any item.This charger is recalled with release# 15-123.They went out for maybe four or five hours and when they returned they first heard their smoke alarm sounding. column11: ShenZhen Global Egrow E-Commerce Co. Ltd
column22: I bought a new Suaoki jump starter off Amazon less than 30 days ago.  I had it in my truck while driving down the road not connected to anything nor plugged in, the unit was turned off.  Without any warning and before I could even react, smoke came billowing out of the unit and before I could blink flames were as high as the dashboard.  It burned up a good portion of the interior of my truck as well as belongings and a burn to my right hand, then there is the smoke damage to the whole interior of the truck.  If I had not reacted so quickly it could have been much more worse.  The more research I have done, it appears that this is a common problem with these mini jump starters that use this type of lithium battery.  I'm more worried that this is going to continue to happen and the outcome could be much worse.  Amazon does not seem to care and has even removed the negative comment, the most they have done is refunded the purchase price.

column6: INSIGNIA 24-inch Class F20 Series Smart HD 720p Fire TV (NS-24DF310NA21, 2020 Model) Tv remote
column22: I purchased an insignia fire stick tv from Best Buy through Amazon and the remote keeps going through batteries faster and faster. Found many complaints after this unsafe incident. I put batteries in they went dead overnight put new ones in remote not working. Set it down and after a few minutes remote started smoking, batteries nearly burnt me when I took them out. I opened remote and can’t find anything on circuit board burnt but the plastic housing is melted in two places. There is a white piece with what looks like a Lens facing the back of the remote on the circuit board. When you put the batteries in it this piece gets hot enough to instantly burn you. I’m glad I didn’t switch to lithium batteries seeing how much more volatile they can be when over heated compared to alkaline batteries. This remote could have burnt my house down.

column6: Lithium Ion Battery for Dell Inspiron 6000 laptop personal computer.Model #: PN111319ASIN: B000GE7E6SLi-ion battery 11.10v 7200mAh
column22: I purchased a replacement laptop battery for my Dell Inspiron 6000 laptop in July, 2010. I bought the battery from Amazon.com. On April 2 at approximately 9:30pm CST the laptop began to smoke, pop and sizzle. The laptop was idle at the time of the incident and no person was in close proximity to the laptop when it caught fire.  I unplugged the laptop, grabbed it by the screen and threw it onto my outdoor wood deck to ensure the house would not fill with more smoke or catch fire. The battery/laptop popped again and a flame appeared. I extinguished the fire with table salt as I was afraid water might have caused an explosion. A second explosion happened and this was also extinguished. I proceeded to throw the laptop to the middle of my grass yard away from my residence. A third explosion occurred at this time and I took dirt and a shovel to cover the laptop and ensure the fire was completely extinguished.Seller: Sun Valley Tek via Amazon.comDescription Li-ion battery 11.10v 7200mAh

column6: This incident could have been caused by the battery and/or the charger, so details for both are listed in this field but it was the battery that exploded so I will devote the fields below to that product only.-PKCELL ER14505 3.7V 2400mAh Rechargeable Lithium Battery,  AA size, Color - solid blue. (minimal info on label, obviously not labelled for resale)-[REDACTED]
column22: A rechargeable lithium battery exploded in my battery charger. No one was injured. There was no subsequent fire. I will provide details about both the battery and the charging unit.The battery was an ER14505 size AA 3.7V Rechargeable Lithium-Ion cell manufactured by PKCELL. I do not know if the cell had a Mfg. date or serial/batch info printed on it because the case was severly damaged in the explosion. The cell had no observable defects before being put into the charger. I did not personally purchase the PKCELL batteries, I acquired them from a releative who no longer needed them.The charger was a [REDACTED]. The charger was purchased by me on February 28, 2024 and had been used about 3 times prior to this incident. This was the first time that I had charged 3.7V ER14505 cells in the unit. Following the incident the charger was intact, with some explosion residue on it. I immediately removed power from the unit, and after reviewing some MSDS information about the potential residue from the explosion the unit has been bagged and retained.There were four of the PKCELL AA batteries in the charger at the time of the incident. The batteries were being charged at a level of 300mA. That charging value was [REDACTED]. I was not injured in the explosion, but seconds before it occured my hand and face were inches away from the unit. I was simply VERY lucky! There was no apparent property damage. I had just checked the progress of the charging unit without touching it, took two steps away and it exploded. Thankfully I was on the opposite side of a large vice on my workbench which shielded me from the blast. It was very loud, generated about a 2 foot diameter fireball and quickly filled my garage with acrid smoke setting off the smoke detector across the room. I set up fans to clear the smoke and left the room as quickly as possible. The three other batteries that were in the charger at the time of the explosion were ejected by the force. One of them landed about 8 feet away behind me. The cells that were in the charger next to the one that exploded were not visibly damaged but have also been bagged and will never be used again. The 8 foot ceiling of my garage and my entire work area was splattered with explosion residue (wet black fragments and dust) which I have been carefully removing.

column6: Reversible Micro USB Cables Omaker 2 Pack Reversible Micro USB Cable High Speed USB 2.0 A Male to Micro B Sync and Charging Cable Cord with Reversible USB Port.
column22: Purchased Omaker 2 Pack Reversible Micro USB Cable High Speed USB 2.0 A Male to Micro B Sync and Charging Cable Cord with Reversible USB Port from Amazon. The cable ends have been modified to allow them to supposedly be inserted into the mating female socket in either orientation - similar to how the [REDACTED] cables or USB C cables work. The first time I plugged the cable in to charge an external battery pack ([REDACTED] power bank) from an AC USB charger ([REDACTED] port wall charger), the cable shorted, the battery quickly overheated, and the cable became melted to both the charger and the battery. The charger eventually shut off, but left my living room filled with the smell of smoke and burning plastic. If the charger had not turned off as quickly as it did, the battery could have easily exploded, as it contains a Lithium Ion battery pack. I feel the modification of an industry standard cable connector type for this function is highly dangerous and inadequate safeguards are built into the product.

column22: I bought a CloudKey Gen2 Plus from Ubiquiti Networks on 1 Jun 20. One of the reasons I bought it was the internal battery would allow the device to gracefully shutdown when power was lost. The device worked for a period of time. Last month (Sep 21) I unplugged the device to make some changes to my network and the device immediately shutdown, it did not gracefully shutdown. Upon investigation, a large number of users of the device were complaining on line of similar issues. A number of users had disassembled their devices and discovered Lithium Ion Battery pack had swollen. These are passively cooled devices which are tightly packed. The battery sits under the CPU which does get quite hot (upwards of 60C. I pulled my device apart and discovered that the batter had swollen as well.This condition presents a significant safety hazard and these devices are meant to be left unattended. if the battery swells and ruptures it could cause a fire. After repeated inquiries Ubiquiti has yet to acknowledge this as an issue.
column39: Ubiquiti Inc: Ubiquiti takes product safety issues very seriously. We welcome resolving any concerns regarding product safety directly with our customers and encourage our customers to provide additional information about incidents to support@ui.com.

column22: Product: Tesla For kids from Radio FlyerIncident: Serious burns and electrical shockWe got this toy today for our 4 year old. He was super excited. The toy has a lithium battery which charging is accessible via a port on the side. This is the main safety issue.It mimics a real Tesla vehicle, but here's the problem. As any toddler, my son thought that he could pretend he was refueling the  car by using the phone jack cable and insert it on the battery socket. It caused a short circuit, that made his hand latch into the cable as he screams in pain asking for help.Luckily I was nearby, at first I thought the car battery caught fire due the smell of plastic, then I realized what happened, the cable melted and the plastic started burning his small hands as he fought to release it but couldn't due the electrical current.This is a serious flaw in design, most electrical toy cars operate on acid batteries but those are hidden under the seat and can only be accessed via a screw removal.This product should be recalled, and none of the electrical components should be exposed like that.

column22: I purchased an electric bicycle back in December from farbike.com. I assembled it as per manufacturer's instructions and took it to a bicycle mechanic to be certain. The design of the bike is such that if you do not attach the battery just right, it will sit imperfectly but still run. I was riding to work one day and the battery fell off the bike. I took it home and attempted to charge and it started sparking, so I undid it and contacted the manufacturer. They asked for it back, so I sent it. Two months later, I finally get a new battery in. The new battery refuses to hold a charge, so I contacted them again, and suggested the charger might have been damaged as well. They sent me a new charger. With a new battery AND new charger, I then went to charge it overnight, as per the instructions. In the morning, I awoke to flashes of light and popping noises; the battery was sparking and jumping from the table I'd set it on. But I could not get close enough to unplug it as the sparks were too powerful. It set my couch, then my walls and entire home on fire and I lost everything. The Alamo Heights Fire Department has a report; they said if I hadn't awoken when I did I probably would not have awoken at all; the smoke and heat rising upstairs to my loft would have choked me.We contacted the company, who passed us off to various sales reps but refused to answer our questions on liability or compensation, then passed us off to a rep in China who claimed to know nothing and has since ignored us. No refund on bike, no replacements, no compensation for thousands of dollars in damages to mine and my landlord's property. This company needs to be held accountable!http://www.farbike.com/x-treme-x-cursion-xb-310li-folding-electric-bike

column22: N. Andover FD was called to [REDACTED] for a remote control airplane's Lithium Polymer battery fire. Remote controlled airplane had been given as a Christmas present to her son [REDACTED] in 2013. The night of the incident was the first time the owner had charged the battery intending to use the plane later that day. He plugged in the wall charger to the battery at approximately 2pm, came back to check on it at 3:15pm and no red charging light was illuminated (flashing or steady). The battery was cold at this time. He unplugged the charger from the wall, then plugged charger back into the wall (Red light flashing now). At 4:00pm the owner noticed no light illuminated again and the battery still cold. This time he unplugged the battery itself from the charger and replugged it back into the charger(Red light flashing now). Owner watched the charging light for 5 minutes then left. The owner checked on the charger every hour till 8:30pm and noticed the charging light still flashing. Owner took a shower, got out, and around 10:00pm he heard a pop in the room the charger was in. Upon investigation by the owner, he noticed the battery on fire. He retrieved a dry chemical extinguisher and used it to extinguish the fire. Owner's sister called 911 to report the fire. The fire was out upon FD arrival. [REDACTED] requested fire investigation unit respond. Fire investigator [REDACTED] responded to investigate.

column22: I received a Bubba Ion cordless fillet knife as a gift for Christmas last year (2020), since then I have used it 4 or 5 times (filleting at least 40 fish in all). The last two times I used it (Thursday July 29TH and Saturday July 31st), the trigger wouldn’t disengage after I removed my finger from it. I had to remove the battery to get the knife to stop. I searched online thinking there was a recall I wasn’t aware of. No recalls but I found plenty of other customer reviews reporting the same trigger issue dating all the way back to March of last year.  The manufacturer is aware of the safety issue because they responded to one of the reviews on their website, and they sent a  a replacement to [REDACTED] the customer that reported the issue in March 2020. So they have known about the issue for close to a year and a half and are still selling the product as is. They wouldn’t even accept a return from a customer in August of 2020 over the same issue. Both of these customers are verified purchasers, and this isn’t a third party seller on Amazon that I’m getting these reviews from. It’s the Bubba store. There’s even a video in one of the reviews (by [REDACTED] verified purchaser) that shows the safety issue in action. Does someone need to be injured before a recall takes place?

column6: Unitron uDirect 2 (FCC ID: VMY-UDIRECT2) is a small black device that hangs around the user's neck and communicates TV and Cell Phone signals via bluetooth with the user's hearing aids. It is powered by a lithium polymer battery (model: AE482539P)
column9: Other Sound Recording, Reproducing or Receiving Equip. (573)
column22: My wife uses a Unitron uDirect 2 bluetooth device to transmit sound from the TV and her telephone to her hearing aids. The uDirect 2 is carried around her neck when she is using it.Three weeks ago she noticed that the back cover on the device would not close. On inspection, the Lithium Polymer Battery (3.7v, 450mAh, 1.67Wh), Model AE482539P, had expanded and caused the back of the device to break open. The battery was in danger of exploding and could cause serious injury.We took the device to Johnson Audiology in Chattanooga where she had received the device less than 2 years ago. They sent the device to Unitron but it was returned with a comment that they do not replace batteries but would sell us a new device (approx $300) or rebuild our old device (approx $150). I called Unitron and they told me they don't deal with consumers and don't sell batteries for their device.Searching the internet for a replacement battery (<$30) I discovered that no direct replacement was available and that many others had had similar problems and at least one other person's battery had exploded. This appears to me to be a serious health and safety issue with this battery and device.
column38: Contacted Johnson Audiology and Unitron

column6: Sony Model No. NP-FX110 DC 7.4V 3800mAh Lithium Ion Rechargeable Battery Pack, UL File NO. E251447. Attached to and sold in conjunction with Sony portable DVD player.
column22: Sony battery pack Model No. NP-FX110 split open while attached to portable dvd player while in use. Upon inspection, one of two 3.7V cells has expanded to approximately 5-7 times the volume of the other (unaffected) cell. Feels squishy to the touch, as if filled with a gas under pressure. The unit was not subjected to trauma or temperature extreme, and was in normal use playing a dvd at the time it expanded. It is suspected that, had the malfunction not been detected, the cell could have burst and spread its contents outside the casing.
column39: Sony Electronics: According to the customer, the Product battery pack split open while in use. There is no report of any injury or property damage occurring at any time.  In an effort to investigate this matter further, and inspect the Product at issue, the customer was contacted by Sony.  Unfortunately, the customer has not responded to Sony’s attempts to contact the customer.  Thus, Sony has not had an opportunity to inspect or examine this Product in more detail, and submits this response based solely on the report the customer provided to the Commission.  . That said, based on the statements made in the Report, Sony recognizes the event similar to an issue that Sony engineers have previously identified as a quality service issue, and the battery pack enclosure casing opening does not pose a safety concern.   Based on the limited information available in the  Report, Sony does not believe that the Product creates an unreasonable risk of injury or contains a defect that could create a substantial product hazard, as defined in Section 15 of the Consumer Product Safety Act, 15 U.S.C. § 2064.  Sony Electronics: Upon examination, investigation, and further consideration of the issue reported related to the battery pack splitting open in a portable DVD player, it has been determined that the product and alleged issue poses no substantial risk of injury to the consumer or property damage, and thus Sony does not believe that any substantial product hazard exists.                                                                                                                                                                                                                               The battery cells in the battery pack can sometimes swell when there is an imbalance in the charge of the cells, which can sometimes lead the battery pack to split slightly open.  However, upon investigation and further examination it has been determined that the battery and its cells are incapable of reaching a temperature that can cause injury or property damage beyond the battery pack or DVD product itself.  This is so even when an imbalance in the charge of cells occurs.   As a result, Sony does not believe that there is a safety concern or any substantial risk of injury here.  This has been reviewed and confirmed by SEL’s Director of Product Safety.

column22: July 13, 2016Dear Sir,	I am the owner of an Apple I-phone 5 which I purchased new approximately 3.5 years ago. It has been well taken care of and only attached to Apple connectors. On July 12, 2016 I took it to an Apple Store and was informed that the lithium battery had a heat anomaly and that caused the screen to lift up. Being concerned that this battery problem (similar to hover boards, laptops, etc.) could cause a fire in my pocket or elsewhere (car, home, etc.), I contacted Apple support on July 13, 2016 to see if this was a safety hazard. If a safety issue, I would expect Apple to correct the situation gratis as consumer safety should be a top priority. The Apple representative, [REDACTED], after again suggesting I purchase a new Apple 5 for $270 informed me that I could have the battery replaced for $80. However I was advised NOT TO USE THE PHONE!!!!. (See correspondence below) This is a dichotomy. Either a safety issue exists or it does not. If a safety issue exists, Apple should immediately correct the problem without charge. If no problem exists, I should be able to use the mobile phone. Since something happened with the lithium battery, I am making you aware of a potential safety issue with the I-phone 5. I am aware that others have had similar battery problems with the I-phone 5 and Apple extended their normal year warrantee. column22: My daughter received a Hover-1 Ranger Hoverboard for Christmas. We plugged it in to charge it for a few hours and then unplugged it, per the operation manual. My daughter rode it around for a bit and then turned it off, leaving it unplugged. We left the house and after a bout 2 hours, my husband got an alert that our smoke detectors were going off at our house. I called my parents to go check on our house because we were 20 minutes away. They entered our house and could immediately see smoke and smell the electrical burning. They found the hoverboard smoking and my dad pushed it out the door with his feet. Within moments it burst into flames. Thankfully no injuries occurred and our house is ok, just has a terrible electrical burn smell. We are attempting to clean our carpets, walls and coats that were nearby that all took the brunt of the smoke. If my parents had gotten to our house even 2 minutes later, our house would have caught fire. This could have been catastrophic. We could have lost our house and our 3 cats.

column6: Ryobi 40V Volt Lithium-ion 6.0 Ah High Capacity Battery, Model 0P40602VNM, Black, 6inL x 3inW x 3inH, used for lawn equipment (mower, string trimmer)
column22: On a Thursday afternoon, we suddenly noticed smoke in our kitchen. We were able to identify quickly that the smoke was coming from one of our cabinets. I opened the cabinet and found smoke forcefully flowing out of one of our Ryobi 40V batteries for our lawn mower and string trimmer. I was able to remove the battery to the yard, where the battery began to actually flame and burn. We called the fire department, who advised us to just let it burn out in a fire pit until it exhausted all of the lithium in the battery. We suffered some minor smoke damage in our cabinet, as well as some melting of the casing of the other two Ryobi 40V batteries that were in the cabinet. The batteries were all purchased new with new equipment in May of 2023, and used within manufacturer guidelines.  The batteries were kept on the charger only when charging, and removed once they were fully charged. We always stored them off the charger in a kitchen cabinet that is dry and maintained at 68-74 degrees year round. The batteries had stored for the winter and had not been disturbed since Oct/Nov of 2023, so there was no apparent event that would cause the battery to suddenly combust. We were lucky (a) to have been home to notice the smoke and remove the burning battery before it caused a house fire, and (b) to have enough time to research how to extinguish the battery and NOT use a standard fire extinguisher or water on it, which actually makes battery fires worse. If either of these things had gone differently, we could have had a lot more damage or injuries.

column1: Razor USA Recalls GLW Battery Packs Sold with Hovertrax 2.0 Self-Balancing Hoverboards Due to Fire Hazard
column3: This recall involves the removable GLW battery packs installed in UL-Listed Hovertrax 2.0 brand self-balancing scooters/hoverboards manufactured between September 2016 and August 2017. GLW's battery packs were certified as compliant with ANSI/UL Standard 2271 by UL. Hovertrax hoverboards have two wheels at either end of articulated dual platforms and are powered by GLW battery packs. The battery packs have the name GLW written in large letters on the packs themselves. The battery packs have serial numbers beginning with GLW, which is printed on a white sticker on the top of the battery pack, below the bar code. Hovertrax 2.0 is printed on the front of the hoverboard. The hoverboards were sold in black, black monochrome, blue, green, red, red monochrome, white, rose gold, camouflage, ocean blue, purple colors, and three multicolor patterns called Spectrum, Galaxy and Carbon.

column22: I purchased a Renogy inverter/charger (R-INVT-PCL1-20111S) on 11/30/2020 as part of a solar power and lithium battery upgrade for my RV.  The first unit that I purchased directly from Renogy was not working correctly, so I purchased a second one from Renogy and also a third from Amazon.  All three inverter/chargers had the same Issue.  There is a circuit within the unit called the Automatic Neutral to Ground bonding circuit that is a safety feature required by UL458 to prevent ground loops and ungrounded AC circuits in mobile homes that is not working correctly.  In fact, it is programmed to work backward, bonding when it should be unbonded and unbonding when it should be bonded.  This caused over $200 worth of damages to my RV, caused the GFCI circuit within my RV to trip constantly and posed the potential for electrocution hazard because it caused 60VAC to be present between ground an neutral of the AC receptacles.  I have since replaced the Renogy inverter/charger with a unit from [REDACTED], a competing manufacturer, which works correctly.  Renogy has denied the hazards I witnessed and has done nothing to correct the problem other than to provide a (yet to materialize) refund for returning the items.  I have lots of email correspondence with them stating UL458 compliance but at the same time discounting the need for this automatic circuit despite it being outlined in their user manual.  I have created videos of the issue:[REDACTED]

column22: Google Nest Protect Gen 2 Smoke/Carbon Monoxide alarm battery overheating. Second time this has happened in my Nest Protects, I have 7 of them. November 2022 issue: Nest Protect reported no issues in regular testing since installed in 2019. Suddenly, it loudly chirped and the app popped up with a low battery warning. I popped it off the ceiling and opened up the battery compartment to swap the batteries and was greeted by the faint smell of what I can only describe as hot plastic/burning rubber. I pulled the batteries out and one of them was BURNING hot. Like boiling water hot - could barely touch it. Battery took hours to cool down outside. Google replaced the device through their safety program. November 2023 issue: Same exact issue, different device. Contacted Nest and am awaiting contact from the safety team again. I used a thermal camera and the hot battery, an [REDACTED] Ultimate Lithium AA battery, was around 150 degrees. Other batteries in the device and the other 6 devices I checked as a precaution were only 70 degrees.Extremely concerning since this is a fire detection device and this could have the potential to cause a fire if the battery got hot enough. After having this happen twice to me and seeing many reports on Google Nest's forum, I felt it appropriate to report to CPSC. See this page for lots of similar reports through the forums: [REDACTED]

column6: UltraFire li ion rechargeable battery
column22: On January 18, 2016 my husband  was in our basement and was preparing to change our car's head light.  He was about to use a flashlight with a lithium ion rechargeable battery that is intended to be worn on one's head.  As my husband was lifting the flashlight to his head he turned on the flashlight and the battery exploded.  The explosion sounded like a bomb.  I was upstairs with my children and thought the noise was one of our basement windows shattering.  I came down to investigate what happened and saw my husband in shock and complaining of ear pain and grabbing his chest.  He had a burn on his chest; the chemicals had gone through his shirt.  Scattered on the basement floor and carpeting were tiny remnants of the battery.  There was a strong odor in the basement.  I contacted the fire department and they removed the pieces of the flashlight and battery.   They took up our carpet and removed it from the basement.  The fire department sent emergency medical staff who advised my husband to go the emergency room.  I brought my husband to the emergency room and he had a chest x ray and his ears examined.  My husband's chest x ray was negative but he was having ringing in his ears.    My husband visited an ear nose and throat physician assistant who ordered a hearing test.  The hearing test results showed considerable hearing loss in my husband's left ear. The physician assistant prescribed oral steroids to my husband and advised him to come back for a follow up appointment in two weeks.  My husband is still having hearing loss in his left ear, ringing in the left ear ,sensitivity to loud noises and generalized headaches.

column22: The product is a Microsoft Surface Pro 4 tablet computer. I noticed what looked like burn marks on the left, right and bottom of the LCD screen and as I was adjusting the kick stand (a leg in the rear of the tablet that allows you to adjust the screen to varying angles for viewing) so that I could be sure it wasn't a reflection I felt and heard the kick stand scrape the rear of the tablet. Upon inspection I found that the rear panel of the tablet was swollen outwards and it was at this time I also noticed the tablet screen was swollen outwards. Upon research of this issue I came across numerous upon numerous complaints from other users of this tablet, the tablets issued before this one and the tablets issued after this one that have the same issue. The issue is that the  lithium-ion are expanding. Microsoft support reported to me that we users should discontinue use of the tablet computer because of risk of fire and/or explosion, however, Microsoft's own document entitled Caring for your Surface battery say's and I quote Battery expansion from deterioration does not present a safety concern and is most often caused by the formation of non-flammable carbon dioxide (CO2) gas., the next sentence say's and I quote,  If you have a device where the battery has expanded visibly beyond the mechanical enclosure, we recommend you stop using the device. You should handle the device with caution to prevent putting pressure on or risk puncturing the battery cell. If you need assistance, contact us and work with a Surface Support Advocate. Since this battery is enclosed without accessibility we the consumers would never know of this safety issue until it is too late.

column6: Future Motions OneWheel Pint X - an electric one-wheeled board vehicle that operates via onboard batteries.www.pintxflaw.com
column22: Unsure where to go for help with a consumer product sold by a predatory company adhering to gross negligence in regard to a manufacturing defect around a dangerously powerful lithium battery.Future Motions sells products under their label OneWheel. One specific model, the OneWheel Pint X, has a well documented manufacturing defect with the 56V onboard battery - specifically the battery's balance lead wires. The plastic battery casing pinches and rubs the wires, causing them to tear and fray. This results in unsafe product operating conditions or complete product failure. Examples of this are product failure while riding, resulting in injury or death. This issue is well documented here: [REDACTED] Consumers have been struggling to get any kind of meaningful assistance from the company, Future Motion. Future Motion continues to deny any wrong-doing. Using scare tactics, they attempt in any way they can to ignore or deflect responsibility. I own an effected product. They told me to mail it to them at my cost to have it inspected with no guarantee they would fix anything. This is after I opened the product and sent them photographic evidence of pinched wiring. Furthermore, they confirmed if I inspected for the defect myself that I would be ineligible for any assistance. That I would need to pay for a new battery.This issue has been going on for several years now with hundreds of people confirming they've had tremendous frustration dealing with Future Motion and their lack of meaningful support on these expensive electric vehicles.Is there anything we as consumers can do to hold this company accountable for this manufacturing defect that they blame on their customers? Really, this should have been a recall years ago.

column6: Winn Solar Powered 80 LED Security Floodlight.Made in China
column22: I purchased and installed a Winn Solar Powered 80 led security floodlight in early Feburary and it has been working until today when I removed it from under the eve above the garage entrance so I could paint the house. When I took it down I noticed that the door where the battery was was bulging out, so I unscrewed the door and found that the battery pack had doubled in size. The battery pack is wrapped in plastic. I thought that possibly gas had built up in the pack and had nowhere to go so I decided to poke a little hole in the plastic wrapping to relieve the pressure. The instant I penetrated the plastic wrapper the battery pack burst into flames and because I had it in my hand I threw the pack out on the driveway. It sat there burning for about 3 minutes before the flames subsided. I believe the battery pack is lithium ion. I am glad I punctured that plastic as I did, because I believe if the pack had been left in the light and had burst while attached to my house, it would have caught the house on fire. That battery was very near to bursting when I took it out. The battery housing and the entire light fixture is plastic and would have provided ample fuel to cause my house to catch fire.I will notify the company that I bought the product from on Monday about this problem. It is the Homesecuritystore.com at 1760 Chicago ave; suite L21, Riverside, CA 92507 phone 888-501 -7870. I still have the light and the burned battery if you need to see it. Let me know if you need it for some reason.

column22: MANSCAPED sells The Handyman Compact Face Shaver.It is described as Portable Mens Travel Facial Hair Groomer, USB-C Rechargeable Electric Razor, Waterproof, Cordless, Dual-Action SkinSafe Long-Hair Leveler & Foil Blades.It is available not only alone, but as a bundle kit other accessories in different combo models.This device almost caught fire or did catch fire in a smoldering manner, releasing toxic plastic fumes throughout the home to which people were exposed. A fire was very possible if there was any combustible material nearby, especially if the would-be-fire had not been caught early by someone with a good sense of smell. Those who are not able to locate or infer the sources of smells may be more at risk of death, property damage, or homelessness by fire.The product comprises a USB-C charging cable, USB-C male charger wall outlet adapter, and the handheld product itself comprising of a grippable rechargeable battery sub-unit (and a cutter-and-foil-shaver sub-unit). While charging with the provided adapter (which has the same voltage and current markings as the battery unit), the battery unit overheated drastically, and over the course of half an hour leaked plastic fumes throughout the building. The product had disintegrated and melted on the battery unit where the USB-C cable connects to the charging circuitry. Charging port had reached sufficient heat that the plastic had melted onto the cable. At this temperature, we do not know if the lithium ion batteries may have been at risk themselves of catching fire.A previous purchase of this product had sporadically-malfunctioning charging, so one can only surmise that the issue is systemic with this model, and perhaps with the manufacturer that MANSCAPED is working with in China, and related charging circuitry of related products.

column39: Manscaped: Manscaped, Inc. submits this General Comment in response to Report No. 20240722-28E7E-2147337808 regarding the matter reported by a 90 year-old individual involving the Handyman™ compact face shaver.  The Report claims smoldering around the charging port during the charging of the unit. The Handyman™ compact face shaver contains a Printed Circuit Board Assembly (PCBA) that provides protection against over-voltage and high temperatures.  When the temperature rises for any reason, the safety trips and protects the battery from further charging or overheating. The description and images provided in the Report evidence that the PCBA worked as designed.  They depict heat-related damage to the base areas of the shaver near the charging port and do not show damage to the battery or motor components.  The Handyman™ has undergone testing and is certified to be in compliance with standards of Underwriting Laboratories (UL).  (UL 60335-2-28, 6th ed. – Issue Date 2018-6-25, rev. 2021-6-28; UL 60335-1, 6th ed. – Issue Date 2016-10-31).   This UL Standard is for Household and Similar Electrical Appliances.  Numerous fire protection, electric shock,  and other safety-related requirements are incorporated into this Standard.  In addition to the design testing and UL certification of the Handyman compact face shaver, additional battery and charging related inspections and testing are performed during and after the manufacturing process. The Handyman™ comes with instructions to customers regarding the use and maintenance of the device.  The product comes with a charging cable only.  It does not come with any adapter.  The instructions identify the types of charging cables and adapters that are suitable for use with the Handyman™ and describes the risks in deviating from those instructions (e.g. improper voltage and damage to the product).   The company cannot certify the performance of any after-market charging cables or adapters.

column39: Upon examination of the pictures and report, it appears that the user failed to follow the written instructions provided with the shaver.  Warning number 8 warns about this type of injury: “Do not use this appliance with a damaged or broken foil, as injury may occur. Before use, make certain blades are undamaged.”  Statement number 3 in the “Quick Tips & Handy Reminders” section instructs, “Replace the foil head and cutter bars every 12 months or if foils are damaged.”Shaver foils are wear-out parts; the amount of use and pressure applied by the user will affect the rate of wear.  Also, if the user drops the unit and the foil is damaged, it will wear more quickly.   On December 13, 2019, we tried to contact the customer via telephone, inquiring about the incident. As of this date, the customer has not responded to our message. In addition, we sent the customer a call tag to retrieve the unit, but we have not received anything. At this point, we are treating the incident as a normal consequence of misuse.

column6: I'm inquiring about a product we own: a Ryobi Model RY14110A 48 volt 20-inch self-propelled lawnmower (Serial# BMP0780024). Our batteries weren't charging and my husband pulled them out. They are bulging and look dangerous. I started researching the internet including Home Depot where we bought it, as they are a Ryobi distributor. Many people on the reviews complained about their batteries not holding a charge, some posted pictures of the bulging, and others stated their batteries leaked acid in the garage. Supposedly a poor charger was the cause but Ryobi didn't contact us. The reviewers also said that Ryobi Customer Service hasn't been helpful, but a Ryobi Facebook e-mail address was. My husband e-mailed them today and they weren't helpful and told him to call Ryobi Customer Service. My husband called Ryobi Customer Service and they basically said since our lawnmower was built in 2011 that we are out of warranty. However, we were never notified of a recall and I don't see one on this website, either. Many reviewers bought new batteries but they just stopped working due to the malfunctioning charger which was causing the batteries to go bad. Some of the Home Depot reviewers had Ryobi reply on the website and suggest they buy a different Ryobi lithium model--even thought they had already spent $400-$500 on this model, plus any battery replacements at $129 a crack. Aren't companies supposed to replace defunct products/issue a recall? Reviewers commented that there was a recall but hardly anyone (including us) knew about this. I put the bulging batteries in a box in our cool basement. With temperatures hitting 90 degrees this weekend, I didn't want to risk an incident in our garage due to overheating. However, I also don't want to get rid of the evidence in case someone wants to see it. I took pictures but the bulging doesn't show as well in the pictures. Please advise if there is an investigation regarding this product and/or the battery charger. Note: If you look on the Home Depot product site this product has been discontinued: http://www.homedepot.com/p/Ryobi-20-in-48-Volt-Cordless-Self-Propelled-Lawn-Mower-DISCONTINUED-RY14110A/202042176. Thank you.
