column6: NiteIze Nitehowl LED SAFETY necklace, light up dog collar available in green,blue, and red colored LEDs.
column22: I purchased NITE IZE NITE HOWL LED SAFETY NECKLACE in the winter of 2020-2021. It's a dog collar that is charged by 3 lithium button batteries. I only used them about a handful of times and just last week noticed that the lights were very dim, so I went to replace the button batteries and upon opening the back, I saw that 2 of the 3 batteries was extremely swollen. I changed the batteries and left the old ones on top of our 3D printer. A few hours later, as I was talking on the phone with a good friend and walking past our 3D printer, I heard what sounded like a gunshot, a huge POP right as I walked past. After I collected my thoughts and verified that i was not hit and that all my windows were in tact, I noticed one of the button batteries had exploded in 3 different locations about my dining room and hallway to my kitchen. Then I noticed 2 different walls that were located next to pieces of the button batteries were damaged by this explosion. It had exploded with such force that it took off paint and made a huge indentation in my kitchen wall and also threw another piece directly into my dining room wall. Luckily, the explosion just merely missed hitting me! If I had not took those batteries out of the collars, 100% they would have exploded and killed my dogs! These should be taken off the shelves at once to prevent this from happening to a sweet innocent pet. I've purchased this from the store Petpeople in Aurora, IL but I'm aware that they are on sale as well currently on [REDACTED], [REDACTED], [REDACTED], [REDACTED], [REDACTED], and list goes on. People need to be aware. I've just read a review from an [REDACTED] customer thar purchased this same product that this has happened to as well. And with their situation, the collars back (that is actually held on by a single screw) had gotten blown off during the explosion and broke the back into several pieces. Luckily for them, it was also not on their dog at the time of explosion.
column38: Purchased from petpeople store in Aurora brand new. Only bought the blue and the green led collars in the year to 1.5years I had it. Just replaced the original batteries. The original batteries were the ones that exploded.

column22: I own a Logitech Harmony 890 universal remote. It appears that many of these devices have a defect Lithium Ion Battery and defective charging circuit that causes the battery to swell. I have had this experience twice. Given what I have learned about Lithium Ion batteries, I am concerned that this represents a fire hazard. The vast number of incidents with this remote are document in this public thread -- http://forums.logitech.com/t5/Share-Your-Thoughts/Swollen-Battery-for-Harmony-890-Remote/td-p/490214While Logitech support has replaced the battery, their own support agents acknowledge that the charging circuits have a defect that results in over charging and damage if the remote is left on the charger for more than 3 hours. Multiple attempts to explain to them that this is a fire hazard has results in no meaningful action beyond the battery replacement. While I appreciate the battery, my main concern is the fire risk many of us face. Below I've included a few key extracts from the long thread of interactions I've had with Logitech's support team.I am submitting this report in the hope that this serious safety hazard can be addressed. Thank you for your help.[REDACTED]----------------------------------------------------------------------------Here is a message where [REDACTED] indicates that leaving the remote in the charger more than 3 hours can damage the charging circuit: Dear [REDACTED],Thank you for contacting Logitech Customer Care here is your incident number [REDACTED] you can use for future reference.My name is [REDACTED]l and I will be your Technical Support Representative for this case.I understand that you are having difficulties with your Harmony 890 remote. I apologize for the inconvenience that this has caused you. and I will gladly assist you with your concern.[REDACTED], I am very sorry for the inconvenience that you have experienced with your Harmony 890 and for having 2 batteries that had thesame problem. Normally those battery that has blue label are the only ones that has the possibility to swell and it doesn't happen on the battery thathas white label. Also if you are not using the remote don't leave it on the charging cradle so we can avoid over charging that can damage the chargingcircuit of the remote. If you have further questions, please reply to this email and we will get back to you shortly. You can use the incident number above for your reference.------------------------------------------------------------------Here is my last message I sent in the support thread, following by their response:[REDACTED], I have attempted in good faith to express my safety concerns. None of the responses have addressed the main issue: If the charging circuit can be damaged by simply leaving the device on the charging cradle, resulting in overcharged batteries, there is a serious safety issue as over charging LION batteries can result in fires. I know this because I have worked with this battery technology in other contexts. The extent of the problem is clearly documented in your forums, including the thread located at http://forums.logitech.com/t5/Share-Your-Thoughts/Swollen-Battery-for-Harmony-890-Remote/td-p/490214/page/6While I appreciate the battery replacement, I am left with the impression that Logitech's solution to the fire safety hazard is to tell people to not leave the remote on the charger. This is not an acceptable safety solution. I am once again requesting that my case the escalated to someone who can discuss & address the core safety issue. If that does not happen shortly I will be left with not other choice than to report the issue to the Consumer Products Safety Commission https://www.saferproducts.gov/CPSRMSPublic/Incidents/ReportIncident.aspxI hope you understand that my goal is to help both Logitech and your consumers. If Logitech has a product in the market that represents such a fundamental safety risk, but does not act to resolve the safety issue, then they are putting both the lives of consumers and the future of the company at risk. . -------Thank you for contacting Logitech Customer Care here is your incident number [REDACTED] for future reference.My name is [REDACTED] and I will be your Technical Support Representative for this case.I understand that you are having difficulties with your Logitech Harmony 890 remote. I apologize for the inconvenience I will gladly assist you with your concern. [REDACTED], I am very sorry for the inconvenience If you have any comments that you would like to direct at our Product Specialists, please feel free to do so within our survey found at www.logitech.com/ithink. They are always looking for ways to improve upon our product and service. Any input you can provide would be greatly appreciated.

column6: Yellow MaxiZum Zumaround electrically motorized scooter/kickbike/foot bike with 26 bicycle wheels, approximately 250 watt, Chinese made geared hub motor, unknown controller, two 36-volt, 9 amp-hour lithium ion bottle batteries that may be made by Panasonic but have no manufacturer ID other than a Zumaround sticker.
column22: I was riding my new MaxiZum electrically motor-driven scooter (with 26 wheels, more properly a footbike or kickbike) westbound in a Class II bike lane when I had to merge into the main traffic lane at a point where the road narrowed to allow for reconstruction of a mid-block crosswalk near our main fire station.It was the Thanksgiving holiday weekend, so no work was in progress. Thee excavation was covered by a smooth steel plate and the transition from the existing pavement to the plate was eased by a gentle, shallow, smooth graded asphalt ramp with no lumps, bumps, gaps or irregularities.The line of traffic was moving very slowly and I had plenty of time to observe the construction work as I approached.  With nearly 60 years of cautious and safe cycling experience, and the knowledge that the footbike had an advertised ground clearance of 4 inches, I judged that the very minor elevation change was perfectly safe to ride over at a slow speed. I was wrong.As my front wheel mounted the incline, two tube ends projecting below the floor frame of the footbike grazed and dug into the asphalt, bringing the MaxiZum to a sudden halt and slightly lifting the rear wheel from the ground and causing it to rotate, airborne, to the right, around the axis of the bike's headset.  I was able to quickly step/jump off to the right and prevent myself from falling all the way to the ground, although my right knee did collapse, resulting in a strained/sprained medial collateral ligament, which is still a bit sore.  following traffic, also moving slowly, was able to stop without hitting me, and I dragged myself and the scooter/footbike off to the side of the road. Not a catastrophe, but it certainly would likely have been if I and/or following traffic had been traveling faster.When I got home, I put the footbike on a bench, removed the footboard, and inspected the (minor) damage to the tubes that had contacted the pavement.  With the footboard removed, I was also able to measure the actual ground clearance of the device.  As can be seen in the photos I took (if I can find a way to share them with you), the tubes that struck the asphalt provides less than 3 of clearance.  Further, a travel-limit tab on the center kickstand measured about 2 5/8  ground clearance, but it also shows signs of having struck the pavement (perhaps when the airborne rear wheel came bouncing and sliding back to Earth), so it's quite likely that the original clearance there was even less.The close examination I was able to do on the bench made it clear that it is not only ground clearance per se that makes this motorized (to approx. 20 mph) device unsafe to operate on US streets and paths with the usual bumps, uneven pavement, projections, etc.  The very design and geometry of the frame makes it dangerous, as well.  The near-90-degree junction of the vertical tube cluster with the floor frame, combined with tube ends and other projections *below* that frame, means that almost any contact with an irregularity or projection from the surface will be, effectively, a head-on crash.  With no leading curve or angle, and with the afore-mentioned projections below the frame, and with no skid plate, there is almost no chance that the footbike would be able ride/slide over even the slightest obstacle.When I contacted the manufacturer, he (trying to prevent me from filing this report, no doubt) promised a detailed written response within a few days.  He then said he was going to Israel for a couple of weeks but would try to write when he was there. From Israel (presumably) he told me he had been busy, but would try to write soon.  When he returned from Israel, he again promised a letter in a few days, which started and email exchange in which he arrogantly assured me that the problem is with my expectations, not with his product.Full disclosure: While waiting for resolution of this issue, I raised it in an online forum for scooter enthusiasts, without identifying the brand or model of the product (it turns out that some of the members of the forum, including the forum operator, are friends/customers of the manufacturer, so they probably knew which product I was talking about).  Virtually every response I got was, more or less That's just how kick scooters are; you have to be constantly alert and ready to stop and dismount at the sight of any obstacle.  I tried to explain that is an unreasonable position to take when talking about a 20 mph motorized footbike, as big as a full-sized bicycle, that is advertised for use on the streets and will likely encounter minor obstacles frequently, and often be surrounded by auto traffic.  They just kept repeating themselves.In the intervening period, I have test-ridden electric footbikes that have none of the inherent defects of the MaxiZum.  They have more than twice the ground clearance.  They have curved/angled junctions between the vertical members and the floor frame.  They have no projections below the floor frame.  They have skid plates.  They would have been ridden safely over the little construction rise that threw me from the MaxiZum without even noticing it.Today, I contacted the seller.  He told me what the manufacturer and the forum members told me: that's just the way kick scooters are.But, no matter how often they call it that, the MaxiZum is NOT a kick scooter -- it's a motorized, full-sized footbike.I appear to be the only person who thinks the device is inherently dangerously flawed, so you may be tempted to just let this go.  Or you may simply not be able to investigate such a minor matter.  However, I am quite certain of this:If very many people ride very many MaxiZum powered footbikes over very many miles of ordinary American roads and trails, there will be serious injuries and, probably, deaths.  How many will simply be a matter of how many scooters he manages to sell and how far the customers ride them.

column38: The manufacturer promised a detailed response to my letter (attached), but kept postponing it and responding to my emails with messages that effectively said this is just the way kick scooters are made; you have to be careful.

column6: Streamlight Twintask 2L
column22: This is a report on a Streamlight TwinTask 2L. Wednesday night, January 23, 2013 I took it with me on a walk and it was not working right.  The next morning (yesterday) I changed the 2 lithium batteries because they tested below 2 volts.  I had 3 fresh [REDACTED] batteries & tested them before inserting them, 1 was under 3v, the other 2 were good & I put the good ones in the light.This is a 3 stage light – switch on once to get bright setting, click again & get that bulb + 3 LEDs or again & LEDs only. The first 2 stages are what quit working; the LEDs worked.  When I tested the batteries I thought the LEDs might have required less power thus they kept working.  But as the bright bulb didn’t light when I replaced the batteries I figured that the bright bulb could have burned out – or that the switch was bad.   I brought the light w/new batteries to the office, put it on my desk. Late afternoon I checked it again, LEDs would only come on very faintly (indicating to me that something had drained the batteries since I installed them).  I went to Streamlight’s website. Saw that this light is discontinued, but I sent an email about having it tested/repaired/replaced. About 30-45 minutes later I was on the phone at my desk when it went bam and shot a 12 flame out the switch hole and kept burning like a flame thrower.  Though the flame shrunk within seconds several papers on my desk were then on fire.  I grabbed the light by the strap, thank goodness for the strap because the light was extremely hot - and took it out the front door where it continued to flame for a couple of minutes. I have probably 6-10 lights with these types of batteries. I’ve gone through probably 50 batteries of this brand over maybe 6-8 years. I don’t mix batteries as they warn. I have had no problem with any other light. I’m suspicious about the switch or circuits in this one – which of course, are now cooked! This is the only light I’ve had until now that had a 3 stage switch, the rest were on/off or brief burst. I have contacted Streamlight, they have requested the flashlight be sent in for forensic testing.  They say it is a battery problem, that they are aware of these problems, but the problems are always battery, not flashlight related.
column38: I have the light, Streamlight has requested it be sent to them w/in 10 days

column6: ANKER POWERHOUSE 767
column22: Product is ANKER POWERHOUSE 767, ordered from seller ankerdirect on EBAY (identifies as FANTASIA TRADING, LLC), apparently a wholly owned subsidiary of ANKER INNOVATIONS LIMITED.  The product is an all in one portable power station (lithium iron phosphate battery pack, inverter, and associated electronics) which controls battery charging and subsequent output of stored energy through an inverter to provide power to household electrical devices which run on alternating current.  It is marketed to people desiring a source of electrical power during a utility power outage (among others).  I first tested the unit by plugging  three devices into it: cable modem, wireless router, and ethernet switch.  The router and switch powered up, the modem ([REDACTED]) did not.  When plugged into a household 120-volt receptacle, the modem powered up normally.  Having extensive electrical experience in my 76 years, I began investigating by plugging in my $5 three-light 120-volt receptacle tester, and found (to my amazement) hot and neutral reversed on this $2000 piece of gear.  An online search of 120 volt receptacle hot neutral reversed will amply describe why this is incorrect and dangerous, so I won't go into that here.  I then searched online forums to see if anybody was talking about such a problem, and found multiple examples of people with the same experience who had made the company aware of the problem, and received replacement units without the problem.  Others who bought units long after the problem was made known to the company, and supposedly fixed, reported receiving units with the problem.  Apparently, the company produced many units, with the problem built into a random subset of the units, due to inadequate instructions and subsequent testing.  Then the units were sent to multiple stock points.  Upon learning of the problem, the appropriate action by the company would have been a recall and stop sale order, since detecting the problem is easy and cheap.  The complicating factors are that the unit weighs 67 pounds, and the packaging is heavy duty and strapped for [REDACTED] shipment, and the multiple stock points.  Opening the shipment units to do the simple test is taxing, so thus far, the company has just continued to ship out units like there is no problem, and replace the problem units as they are identified and returned.  The problem with this approach is that many devices will operate in what appears to be normal fashion, but with the risk of electrical shock that comes with the reversed leads.  The typical customer may have no devices (as I did) that check for the reversal and refuse to power up.  The typical customer will have no experience that leads them to look for such a problem, and no tools to identify it, and will suffer the risk without any warning.

column22: I was using a vibrator I recently purchased off of Amazon when there was a loud pop, a flash of light, and the products sealed top where the adjustment settings button was exploded open and began smoking. The product is covered in rubber or silicone, and it began to melt and was very hot and sticky to the touch. The battery seemed to have exploded inside the product. I put the vibrator on the on the windowsill because it continued to smoke and it began to melt, filling my room with the smoke and smell of melting rubber. It continued to smoke and melt for awhile. I believe this product is dangerous, because though I did not suffer any injuries, the product is intended for use on the body and exposure to sensitive organs. I was very grateful I was not injured, but I believe this product could cause serious injuries in the future.  I believe it could cause burns to the skin, fires, and potential internal or external injuries if the item explodes while it is in use in a manner consistent with the nature of the product (it is reasonably foreseeable someone may insert this product into their vagina, rectum, etc.) I believe because of the nature of this product could also create psychological stress if the product explodes during use even if there is no physical injury.I was using the product externally on my skin as directed. I did not insert the product vaginally, I used the charger that came with the item at all times, and I had no indication that this was a potential danger from normal use of the product. It is available at this link: https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CR95B6HR?psc=1&ref=ppx_pop_dt_b_product_details#customerReviews - I no longer have the packaging.

column6: Patriot Power Generator
column22: May, 13, 2016To: Consumer Products Safety CommissionFrom: [REDACTED]Re: Patriot Power Generator RecallTo Whom It May Concern:Attached please find pdf scans showing that I purchased a Patriot Power Generator from Patriotpowergenerator.com a.k.a. Power4Patriots. They indicate their mailing address as: POB 121709, Nashville, TN 37212, with phone number 800-680-8504.On November 15, 2014 I purchased a Patriot Power Generator for a price of $1997.00. This unit is a battery-powered rechargeable power generator that can be recharged either by pre-charging it using an AC outlet OR with the included solar panels. The unit arrived in what I believed to be normal working condition & was then stored after completing an initial battery charge as directed by the enclosed instructions. I did not use the generator after the initial charge. I have not been physically injured by the product.On February 24, 2016 I received both an email & mailed letter indicating that the referenced generator was being recalled because the lithium batteries posed a fire hazard. The seller's communications advised that they are cooperating with the manufacturer (Lekker, LLC of Lindon, Utah) as they implement a recall with the Consumer Products Safety Commission. I contacted the seller with my serial number & they confirmed that my generator was included on the recall list. They then advised me that they would contact me soon to arrange for return of the unit for a full refund & that the return shipping would be at no charge to me. During the initial call I advised the seller that the shipping box arrived with multiple slashes, thereby preventing me from returning it in the original shipping box. I also advised that I had found a very sturdy box and packing material that would be more than adequate for the return. They advised that they would be responsible for all aspects of the return, including shipping costs & providing a box if necessary.When I had not heard back from the seller by the first week of May, I called the number they provided. During that call, I received conflicting information from the seller's customer service representative. The responses included that the CPSC was still processing the recall, then I was told that the CPSC needed to give them authorization to have the units shipped back to the seller.As the seller's customer service representative seemed to be confused in her responses, I asked to speak with a manager. I was advised that a manager from the seller's company would call me back within 48 hours. To date, I have not heard back from anyone associated with the seller. Instead they mailed me a complimentary packet of instant coffee, while apologizing for the ongoing delay.A few days ago I called the CPSC. The CPSC representative was very helpful. She first checked into the recall & was unable to find any such matter either under the seller or manufacturer's names. She then advised me to send this email, including scans of any documentation associated with the purchase of the generator & the recall. The names that the CPSC representative investigated included: Patriotpowergenerator.com; Power4Patriots; Lekker, [REDACTED]. I also later noticed the name of [REDACTED] of Shenzhen, China.I would appreciate your help to resolve this matter. My goal is to return the unit at no cost to me & the seller should also provide me with a full refund. I would NOT be interested in any type of repair or exchange as I no longer have faith in the seller.Sincerely,[REDACTED]Telephone: [REDACTED]Email [REDACTED]

column6: Surface Pro 4 , with an i7 processor, 16 GB of RAM, and 512 GB of Storage
column22: I recently began noticing yellowish-brownish areas on the left and right side of the display on my Surface Pro 4 device, predominately on the left side.  This seemed to be heat related, since it occurred more, and the discolored area became larger, when the device was running hot, with the cooling fan constantly running at what appeared to be the maximum speed.  Eventually, it was noticed that the left side of the display bulged out and became separated from the frame.  I then began researching this further and filed a service request/case with Microsoft under the category/issue type drop down selection of Lifted, separated, or bulging screen.  After multiple back and forth communications with Microsoft as part of the service request/case and additional research in regards to this I came to understand that the Lifted, separated, or bulging screen is due to the battery expanding, which separated the display from the frame.  Microsoft responded with a boiler plate response, which has contradictory language within it:  in one sentence they state there is “no inherent risk or safety concern associated with these expanded batteries” and then immediately follow it with “the lithium ion batteries used in Surface devices contain materials that can injure skin, eyes, and lungs if punctured” and “Surface devices with expanded batteries should not be connected to AC power as it could possibly lead to rupture;” which is not a misuse according to their labeling, i.e., you should be able to use the device while connected to AC power/a docking station (this is predominately how we use the device), however, a product design flaw created a condition where it cannot be used while connected to AC power/a docking station due to a safety concern according to them.The device is now unusable, according to Microsoft, while plugged in due to a safety concern given the potential for the expanded battery to rupture.  Additionally, it can’t be used when it is unplugged given the potential for a safety concern with the battery, i.e., it can’t be carried around, left powered on unattended, taken on a plane, etc., given the state of the battery and the potential for it to continue to expand and rupture, or to rupture in its current expanded state.  This is a safety issue that is still occurring with product in the field and it needs to be addressed.  I have seen numerous complaints online in regards to this issue.  In some cases it was stated that the device/battery “caught fire and exploded,” evident from images that were provided in that case as well.  Additionally, Microsoft knows this is an unsafe condition due to the expanded battery condition regardless of whether or not it is being charged or discharged, i.e., being used, given that they stated if the device was returned for replacement a special return box must be used for shipping the damaged Surface.They have chosen to deal with this unsafe condition by putting out the boiler plate statements previously referenced (only after a customer has brought their particular occurrence to Microsoft's attention; customers should be made aware of this before it happens, without having to research the condition and finding out about the unsafe condition on their own), and by trying to get some defective product out of the field by replacing it if it is three years and under, where the time period was based on profit/loss and not on safety.  I am hoping that Microsoft will eventually get to a safety recall in regards to this, hopefully before there are additional occurrences of batteries/devices exploding and catching fire, and before anyone gets hurt.  The service request/case that I have filed with Microsoft is still open and continuously being escalated since they will not replace the failed and unsafe device without charging a substantial fee.  I just want the ability to use a safe product, a product that I paid significant money for, for longer than four years.  Especially now, when this product (a PC) is needed more than ever.  I don’t think that is too much to ask for.

column6: Apple MacBook Pro 17-inch widescreen notebook's OEM/stock Apple Rechargeable Battery
column22: I bought two Apple MacBook Pro 17-inch laptops (Macintosh) for personal and office use.Both of these computers came with the standard OEM factory parts, including the Apple-branded rechargeable lithium ion battery, as it should.What should NOT happen is the following: In the 2 years of owning them, I have always exercised safe and very gentle operating and maintenance of the computers (I BABY THEM; I know how sensitive and delicate electronics can be, plus I never want to get even a scratch on the nice aluminum body).I followed all the guidelines in the printed and electronic documentation included with the computers (including the user guide/manual). I always have and always do.HOWEVER I noticed one day that the trackpad (built in touch pad) was difficult to operate and sometimes clicking was especially difficult. Soon I felt the computer was rocking back and forth. So I looked underneath and saw that the rechargeable battery had expanded/swollen out of its normal size. I knew something was wrong so I took it in to the genius bar at the Apple Store in Santa Monica, California (Third Street Promenade), where they brushed this incident off as normal and the battery does that when it is consumed and no longer works. Time to buy a new battery. I replied that the battery still holds a solld 3 to 4 hour charge, and that System Profiler reports the battery status as Normal as far as charge-holding and discharging capacity. It was working fine for what it was made for, but not working fine as far as my computer's keyboard and trackpad operation. They suggested I spend $142 on a new battery (129 + 10.25% Tax). Or buy a bulk packaged battery from the genius bar for only $100. I denied their sales pitch and said that this was a manufacturing defect, and that I was worried it might further expand and burst. Isn't this a health and safety issue? can't this cause a fire, and therefore possible damage and even death? They didn't know what to say. I repeated that I would like a replacement free of charge for this manufacturing defect. They said no. So I left and continued to use the computer. The battery held a charge just fine. Well the next morning I came to my Office and the battery had indeed burst open, spewing some kind of chemical/gook all over my desk. It smelled like burning metal. I took it back to the genius bar, and they again said this was normal and that Apple is not responsible for it. Because my computer was out of warranty they felt they had no need to replace the now exploded Apple battery. A couple months later, I removed the Apple battery from my other 17-inch MacBook Pro, as I was leaving town for a couple weeks and feared a repeat of it blowing up in my house, possibly burning down the building. I placed in on my office desk and  when I came back from my trip, amazingly, the removed battery had somehow swollen up to the point of bursting the metal battery casing apart. No goo/liquid escaped this time, but the product was clearly unusable. I can see the cells inside bloated up. This is clearly not normal. This is a manufacturing defect that Apple is trying to cover up. If you google the phrase Apple MacBook Pro Battery exchange or even Apple Macintosh PowerBook battery recall fires, you will see a lot of this happening in the past, and you'll see that up until 2008 or 2009, Apple admitted fault in their OEM laptop/notebook batteries, and said there is a risk of fire. They offered to replace such batteries for certain models/years, free of charge, no questions ask, regardless of Warranty status. This program for MacBook Pros (same model year as mine) is now closed. you can see the page still on Apple.com if you search the above. But they knew about the issues and HAD a recall, but ONLY for 15-inch MacBook Pros (as opposed to my 17-inch MB Pro), and they decided that they weren't responsible for any of them explooding, burning, catching fires, possibly destroying homes and possibly claiming human lives. Why? They fessed up before!I called AppleCare (Customer Relations) and they interviewed me with a barrage of questions, it felt like a court deposition. They said they'd get back to me on what their engineers find. I said okay, but never heard back from them. I called and left several kind but persistant messages. No callback, no reply, no replacement battery. No apology for possibly burning my house down while i slept. APPLE, YOU NEED TO DO THE RIGHT THING LEGALLY, MORALLY, and BEYOND. REPLACE ALL SWOLLEN BATTERIES FOR FREE WITHOUT HASSLE. DON'T LET THESE normal BATTERIES EXPLODE ON ANYONE ELSE.Apple should issue a RECALL and offer one of their EXTENSION REPAIR PROGRAMS on their site apple.com and/or issue a full refund to owners who have already been FORCED to buy a new battery from Apple at almost $150 after tax per battery.I thank you, APPLE COMPUTERS INC., for correcting this mistake in all fairness, and in accordance with U.S. Consumer Protection Laws.
column38: Still have it because they wouldn't replace it and I refuse to pay $129 plus tax for a defective part that could have caused infinite harm to me and my family. Contacted Apple, they don't care.

column6: The product is a **Future Motion Onewheel GT-S Series**, a personal electric mobility device designed for outdoor use. It has a single large wheel in the center, flanked by footpads on either side for balance and control. The Onewheel GT-S is marketed as an all-terrain electric skateboard capable of handling various surfaces, including pavement, grass, and light off-road environments. It is primarily used for recreational purposes and commuting.The board is **black and gray in color**, approximately **9.5 inches wide, 29 inches long**, and weighs around **35 pounds**. It is powered by a built-in lithium-ion battery and a motor housed inside the wheel. The GT-S model features a digital control system, including self-balancing technology and feedback via pushback and haptic buzz to alert riders of various operating conditions.
column22: I am reporting a serious safety issue with the Onewheel, a personal electric mobility device manufactured by Future Motion. I experienced two significant crashes while using the product, resulting in physical injuries and loss of confidence in the products safety.**Incident 1:**I was riding the Onewheel over wet concrete, navigating shallow puddles as intended for outdoor use. While traveling at approximately 15 mph, the boards self-balancing system suddenly stopped working. The nose of the board dove into the ground, throwing me forward onto my right side. I sustained road rash on my leg, hand, arm, and shoulder, and a broken arm. My helmet also cracked upon impact. After the crash, the board was unresponsive and had condensation inside the casing, indicating water had entered the control board. The Onewheel is marketed as an outdoor product, and failure in such conditions raises serious safety concerns.**Incident 2:**After paying $180 to ship the board back to Future Motion for repairs, I received it and rode it once. During that ride, while stopped at a red light, the board unexpectedly accelerated on its own without any input from me. There was no haptic buzz, pushback, or other indication that the board would accelerate. This malfunction threw me off the board, re-injuring my already broken arm and furthering my distrust in the product. I had only ridden the board a short distance after receiving it from Future Motions repair shop.**Product Information:**The product is a personal electric mobility device known as a Onewheel, which is designed for outdoor use on various terrains. It was being used by me, an experienced rider, in everyday outdoor conditions.**Injuries:**Both incidents resulted in physical injuries. The first incident caused road rash and a broken arm, while the second crash reactivated my previous injuries. The second incident highlights an ongoing issue with the products reliability, posing a serious risk of further injury.**Conclusion:**These incidents suggest that the Onewheel has significant safety flaws. The first crash was due to water exposure despite claims that it is built for outdoor use, while the second crash occurred after the board had been serviced by the manufacturer. Given the physical injuries Ive experienced, this product presents a serious risk to consumers.

column22: While charging the battery GigaByke Groove 48V 14Ah Silicone Battery I noticed that it was becoming extremely hot, so I disconnected the charger and let it cool.  I then attempted to charge it again from another outlet and the same situation occurred.  I removed the batter from the bike and attempted to charge is separately and again the same situation occurred, very hot.  I noticed bulging at the seams of the battery and now feel it is unsafe and a possible fire hazard.  I have contacted the distributor and asked for support, there recommendation is replaced a lightly used battery with a replacement from the same manufacturer for $399.00.  This does not seem like a responsible type of support for an expensive product.  If I did not disconnect the battery from the charger, it could catch fire or explode.

column6: HD Hudson illu-Mist 40001 one-gallon & 40002 two-gallon battery-powered garden sprayers. Model illu-Mist 40001 one-gallon & 40002 two-gallon battThis recall involves illu-Mist 40001 one-gallon & 40002 two-gallon battery-powered garden sprayers.  The sprayers have a plastic triangle-shaped handle in gray with teal blue trim and a white tank with “illu-Mist” printed on the front side of the tank.  The sprayers measure 15.5 inches and 19.5 inches in height when assembled.   Remedy:Consumers should immediately stop using the recalled battery-powered sprayers, remove the battery tray, and look for a brown battery.  If the battery is brown, consumers should remove the battery and tray and dispose of them in accordance with local laws for disposal of lithium-ion batteries, and contact HD Hudson for a free replacement battery and tray.  Always follow proper battery use and storage procedures as listed in the instructions.Incidents/Injuries:The firm has received four reports of batteries overheating, including one report of a battery melting and catching fire resulting in less than $100 in property damage.
column22: Illu-Mist 40001 one-gallon & 40002 two-gallon battery-powered garden sprayers was stored in our bar/barn/garage storage area. It was used to spray disinfectant chemicals in and around our home, bar,garage,and BnB. July 7th 2020 a fire developed at 1:20 a.m (according to security cameras) in our storage area of the bar/barn/garage. The fire spread in a matters of minutes destroy our three story 3800 sq ft Bourbon Bar and garage/barn. It consumed over 350 bottles of vintage bourbons,500 bottles of wine and all the contents of the bar (furnishings etc). Three collector cars ( [REDACTED])and many other valuables. 23 very large trees along with several hundred feet of privacy fencing were destroyed. Many pictures can be provided of the destruction.The cause of fire was never fully discovered and was passed on as possible lightening. But, there were no storms in area for several hours prior to fire. Plus it was estimated that the fire started in storage area where the battery powered sprayer was stored.Too coincidental that the sprayer was in there and now receive a notification that the sprayer is defective and may cause fire.

column3: The U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) and The TJX Companies Inc. are warning consumers that T.J. Maxx, Marshalls and HomeGoods sold 19 different recalled products after they were recalled between 2014 and 2019. This involves about 1,200 units of recalled products sold to consumers. Consumers should stop using the recalled products immediately and contact the recalling firms to receive the remedy listed in the recall, which is either a refund, replacement or repair. column22: As I was mowing my yard this past weekend, the mower suddenly ceased operation and began emitting smoke after approximately 10 minutes of use. I am now collaborating with RYOBI to investigate the root cause of this alarming incident.
